| Density of water at 20 °C | $\rho$ | **998 kg/m³** |
| Dynamic viscosity of water at 20 °C | $\mu$ | **1.002 × 10⁻³ N·s/m²** |
| Kinematic viscosity of water at 20 °C | $\nu$ | **1.004 × 10⁻⁶ m²/s** |
| Lower Critical Reynolds Number | $Re_{c1}$ | **2000 (dimensionless)** |
| Upper Critical Reynolds Number | $Re_{c2}$ | **4000 (dimensionless)** |

> [!TIP]
> **Examination Tip:** Whenever $\nu$ is not given in the problem, use $\nu = 1.0 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}$ for water at standard lab temperature (~20 °C). The 2% error from neglecting temperature correction is acceptable for KTU lab viva and numerical problems.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation — Osborne Reynolds' Framework

Osborne Reynolds (1883) established that the **nature of flow inside a pipe is governed entirely by a single dimensionless group** — the **Reynolds Number** — which represents the ratio of **inertial forces** to **viscous forces** inside the fluid.

$$\text{Re} = \frac{\text{Inertial Forces}}{\text{Viscous Forces}} = \frac{\rho V D}{\mu} = \frac{V D}{\nu}$$

### 2.1.1 Physical Interpretation of Each Term

| Symbol | Meaning | Role in the Ratio |
|---|---|---|
| $\rho$ | Mass per unit volume | Carries the inertia of the moving fluid |
| $V$ | Mean velocity (m/s) | Measures how fast the fluid is being carried |
| $D$ | Pipe diameter (m) | Characteristic length — the size of the conduit |
| $\mu$ | Dynamic viscosity (N·s/m²) | Internal friction resisting motion |
| $\nu = \mu/\rho$ | Kinematic viscosity (m²/s) | "Momentum diffusivity" — combines friction and inertia |

### 2.1.2 Step-by-Step Logic of Flow Transition

**Step 1 — Establish a Flow Baseline.**
Water enters a horizontal, transparent glass tube from a constant-head overhead tank. The flow rate is regulated by a downstream control valve, so the experimenter can dial any velocity they wish.

**Step 2 — Inject a Tracer Dye.**
A dye (typically potassium permanganate solution or methylene blue) is introduced through a thin hypodermic needle at the **centreline of the inlet** of the glass tube. The dye marks a single streamline.

**Step 3 — Observe Dye Behaviour.**
- At very low velocity, the dye travels as a **straight, unbroken, hairline thread** down the entire length of the tube. This is **Laminar Flow**.
- As velocity is gradually increased, the dye line remains straight up to a point and then **suddenly begins to oscillate laterally in a sine-wave pattern**. This oscillation onset marks the **Upper Critical Velocity** $V_{c2}$.
- If the velocity is then *decreased* back down, the dye oscillation disappears at a *lower* velocity than where it first appeared. The velocity at which the oscillating dye finally settles back into a **perfectly straight thread** is the **Lower Critical Velocity** $V_{c1}$.

**Step 4 — Measure the Flow Rate at $V_{c1}$.**
At the moment the dye thread is just steady and straight, water is collected at the outlet in a measuring cylinder for a fixed time $t$, giving discharge $Q$. Velocity is then computed as $V_{c1} = Q/A$.

**Step 5 — Compute the Reynolds Number and Verify.**
Plug $V_{c1}$ into the Reynolds equation and confirm the experimental value is near **2000**.

### 2.1.3 The "Why" Behind the Two Critical Velocities — Hysteresis

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** The difference between the lower and upper critical velocities is called **flow hysteresis**. It occurs because once turbulence begins, the small eddies already in the fluid sustain the chaotic motion even when conditions are slightly below the original triggering point. The fluid "remembers" being disturbed. This is why the transition is a *range*, not a single number.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units | When to Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $Re = \dfrac{\rho V D}{\mu}$ | Reynolds Number (dynamic form) | dimensionless | When $\rho$ and $\mu$ are given |
| 2 | $Re = \dfrac{V D}{\nu}$ | Reynolds Number (kinematic form) | dimensionless | When $\nu$ is given directly |
| 3 | $V_c = \dfrac{Re_c \cdot \nu}{D}$ | Critical Velocity from critical $Re$ | m/s | Forward calculation |
| 4 | $D_c = \dfrac{Re_c \cdot \nu}{V}$ | Critical Diameter from given $V$ | m | Pipe-sizing problems |
| 5 | $V = \dfrac{Q}{A} = \dfrac{4Q}{\pi D^2}$ | Mean velocity from discharge | m/s | Converting measured $Q$ to $V$ |
| 6 | $Q = \dfrac{V \cdot t}{\text{(volume collected)}}$ | Inverse: $Q$ from collecting jar | m³/s | Experimental $Q$ calculation |
| 7 | $V_{c1} = \dfrac{2000 \cdot \nu}{D}$ | **Lower** critical velocity | m/s | Conservative safe design |
| 8 | $V_{c2} = \dfrac{4000 \cdot \nu}{D}$ | **Upper** critical velocity | m/s | Conservative turbulent design |
| 9 | $\nu = \dfrac{\mu}{\rho}$ | Kinematic viscosity definition | m²/s | Unit conversion |
| 10 | $A = \dfrac{\pi D^2}{4}$ | Pipe cross-sectional area | m² | Velocity → discharge conversion |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Syntax Rule for KTU:** In board examinations, always write $Re_c$ (with subscript $c$) for the critical Reynolds number, and explicitly state whether you are using the lower value (2000) or the upper value (4000). Vague answers like "Re is around 2300" earn zero marks in 14-mark derivations.

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

The determination of critical velocity is **not an academic curiosity** — it is the single most important preliminary check in virtually every pipe-flow design:

1. **Water Supply Networks:** Engineers must ensure distribution pipes operate in the **laminar or smooth-turbulent** zone to minimise pumping costs. Knowing $V_{c1}$ allows them to size the pipe so that maximum daily demand does not push the flow into inefficient transitional flow.

2. **Oil and Gas Pipelines:** Crude oil is far more viscous than water (lower $\nu$ in the denominator of $Re$ is offset by very high $\mu$). A wrong assumption about flow regime leads to **catastrophic pressure drop miscalculations**.

3. **Heat Exchangers:** Correlations for the convective heat transfer coefficient (Nusselt number) are completely different in laminar and turbulent regimes. A regime misclassification causes the heat exchanger to undersize by 50–300%.

4. **Sewage and Slurry Lines:** The flow must be kept above a *minimum* velocity to prevent solid settlement. This minimum is itself a function of the critical velocity concept.

5. **Biomedical Devices (Catheters, Stents):** Blood flow must remain laminar to avoid haemolysis and platelet damage; critical velocity defines the safe flow-rate ceiling.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Reynolds Number from Dimensional Analysis

The Reynolds Number can be rigorously derived by collecting all variables that govern pipe flow and forming the dimensionless $\pi$-groups.

### Step 1 — Identify the Governing Variables

From physical reasoning, the pressure drop $\Delta P$ across a pipe of length $L$ carrying a fluid of density $\rho$ and dynamic viscosity $\mu$ at mean velocity $V$ is governed by:

$$\Delta P = f(D,\ L,\ V,\ \rho,\ \mu)$$

That is, six physical variables in total (including $\Delta P$ itself).

### Step 2 — Choose Repeating Variables

Following the Buckingham $\pi$-theorem, we need $n - k$ dimensionless groups, where $n = 6$ and $k = 3$ (the number of fundamental dimensions: M, L, T). So we expect $6 - 3 = 3$ dimensionless $\pi$-groups.

Choose the repeating variables as $D$, $V$, and $\rho$ (they collectively span M, L, and T).

### Step 3 — Form the $\pi$-Groups

$$
\begin{aligned}
\pi_1 &= \frac{\Delta P}{\rho^a V^b D^c} \\
\pi_2 &= \frac{\mu}{\rho^a V^b D^c} \\
\pi_3 &= \frac{L}{D}
\end{aligned}
$$

For $\pi_1$, enforce dimensional homogeneity $[\Delta P] = M L^{-1} T^{-2}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
[\rho^a V^b D^c] &= (M L^{-3})^a (L T^{-1})^b (L)^c \\
&= M^a \cdot L^{-3a + b + c} \cdot T^{-2b}
\end{aligned}
$$

Equating exponents of M, L, T:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Mass (M):} \quad & a = 1 \\
\text{Time (T):} \quad & -2b = -2 \Rightarrow b = 1 \\
\text{Length (L):} \quad & -3a + b + c = -1 \Rightarrow -3 + 1 + c = -1 \Rightarrow c = 1
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore $\pi_1 = \dfrac{\Delta P}{\rho V^2}$, the **Euler Number** (pressure coefficient).

For $\pi_2$, $[\mu] = M L^{-1} T^{-1}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{M (M):} \quad & a = 1 \\
\text{T (T):} \quad & -2b = -1 \Rightarrow b = \tfrac{1}{2} \\
\text{L (L):} \quad & -3a + b + c = -1 \Rightarrow -3 + 0.5 + c = -1 \Rightarrow c = 1.5
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore $\pi_2 = \dfrac{\mu}{\rho V^{1/2} D^{3/2}}$. To obtain the conventional Reynolds Number, we take its reciprocal and square it:

$$
\frac{1}{\pi_2^2} = \frac{\rho V D}{\mu} = Re
$$

### Step 4 — Write the Functional Relationship

$$
\frac{\Delta P}{\rho V^2} = \phi\!\left( \frac{\rho V D}{\mu},\ \frac{L}{D} \right)
$$

In a fully developed pipe flow, the pressure drop is **linear with length**, so the function further reduces to:

$$
\Delta P = \frac{4 \rho V^2 L}{D} \cdot f(Re)
$$

This is the celebrated **Darcy–Weisbach equation**, with $f(Re)$ being the friction factor. The function $f(Re)$ behaves **completely differently** in the laminar and turbulent regimes — which is exactly why identifying $Re$ is so critical.

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical — Finding Critical Velocity

> **Problem:** Water at 20 °C flows through a glass tube of internal diameter $D = 25$ mm. Find (a) the lower and upper critical velocities, and (b) verify the corresponding Reynolds numbers. Take $\nu = 1.0 \times 10^{-6}$ m²/s.

### Part (a) — Critical Velocities

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{c1} &= \frac{Re_{c1} \cdot \nu}{D} \\
&= \frac{2000 \times 1.0 \times 10^{-6}}{25 \times 10^{-3}} \\
&= \frac{2 \times 10^{-3}}{2.5 \times 10^{-2}} \\
&= 0.08 \text{ m/s} \quad = 8 \text{ cm/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{c2} &= \frac{Re_{c2} \cdot \nu}{D} \\
&= \frac{4000 \times 1.0 \times 10^{-6}}{25 \times 10^{-3}} \\
&= \frac{4 \times 10^{-3}}{2.5 \times 10^{-2}} \\
&= 0.16 \text{ m/s} \quad = 16 \text{ cm/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Part (b) — Reynolds Number Verification

$$
\begin{aligned}
Re_{c1} &= \frac{V_{c1} \cdot D}{\nu} = \frac{0.08 \times 0.025}{1.0 \times 10^{-6}} = 2000 \;\; \checkmark \\
Re_{c2} &= \frac{V_{c2} \cdot D}{\nu} = \frac{0.16 \times 0.025}{1.0 \times 10^{-6}} = 4000 \;\; \checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

---

## 3.3 Reverse Worked Numerical — Finding the Critical Diameter

> **Problem:** An engineer must size a pipe to deliver oil of kinematic viscosity $\nu = 4.5 \times 10^{-5}$ m²/s at a flow rate such that mean velocity will be $V = 1.2$ m/s. What is the **smallest diameter** that will keep the flow strictly laminar?

### Step 1 — Apply the Laminar Criterion

For strictly laminar flow, $Re \le 2000$:

$$
Re = \frac{V D}{\nu} \le 2000
$$

### Step 2 — Solve for the Limiting Diameter

$$
\begin{aligned}
D &\le \frac{2000 \cdot \nu}{V} \\
D &\le \frac{2000 \times 4.5 \times 10^{-5}}{1.2} \\
D &\le \frac{9.0 \times 10^{-2}}{1.2} \\
D &\le 0.075 \text{ m} = 75 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Interpretation

The **maximum allowable pipe diameter** to stay laminar at $V = 1.2$ m/s is 75 mm. If the engineer chooses an 80 mm pipe, the flow will be in the transitional zone — risky and unpredictable.

---

## 3.4 Experimental Worked Numerical — From Collected Volume to Reynolds Number

> **Problem:** In a Reynolds apparatus with tube diameter $D = 20$ mm, water is collected in a measuring jar at the moment the dye thread is just steady and straight. Volume collected: $V_o = 250$ mL in time $t = 30$ s. Take $\nu = 1.0 \times 10^{-6}$ m²/s. Determine the experimental lower critical Reynolds number.

### Step 1 — Compute Experimental Discharge

$$
Q = \frac{V_o}{t} = \frac{250 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^3}{30 \text{ s}} = 8.333 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}
$$

### Step 2 — Compute Cross-Sectional Area of the Tube

$$
A = \frac{\pi D^2}{4} = \frac{\pi \times (0.020)^2}{4} = 3.1416 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2
$$

### Step 3 — Compute Mean (Experimental Critical) Velocity

$$
V_{c1,\ \text{exp}} = \frac{Q}{A} = \frac{8.333 \times 10^{-6}}{3.1416 \times 10^{-4}} = 0.02652 \text{ m/s}
$$

### Step 4 — Compute Experimental Reynolds Number

$$
\begin{aligned}
Re_{c1,\ \text{exp}} &= \frac{V_{c1,\ \text{exp}} \cdot D}{\nu} \\
&= \frac{0.02652 \times 0.020}{1.0 \times 10^{-6}} \\
&= 530.4
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5 — Interpret the Result

The experimental value (530) is **much lower** than the standard 2000. This indicates either:
- The water was warmer than 20 °C (reducing $\nu$ reduces the experimental $Re$),
- The "just-straight" observation was made at a velocity well below the true transition,
- Or the tube had non-uniform bore.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** A common student error is to divide $D$ by $\nu$ without first computing $V$. Always go $V = Q/A \rightarrow Re = VD/\nu$ in that order. Reversing the order produces a numerically meaningless answer that earns zero methodology marks.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Decision Matrix — What Formula to Use When

| Given Quantities | Required Quantity | Formula Path |
|---|---|---|
| $V$, $D$, $\nu$ | $Re$ | $Re = \dfrac{V D}{\nu}$ |
| $Re$, $D$, $\nu$ | $V_c$ | $V_c = \dfrac{Re \cdot \nu}{D}$ |
| $Q$, $D$, $\nu$ | $Re$ | $V = \dfrac{4Q}{\pi D^2}$, then $Re = \dfrac{V D}{\nu}$ |
| $Re_c$, $V$, $\nu$ | Critical diameter $D_c$ | $D_c = \dfrac{Re_c \cdot \nu}{V}$ |
| $V$, $D$, $\mu$, $\rho$ | $Re$ (dynamic form) | $Re = \dfrac{\rho V D}{\mu}$ |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Reynolds Apparatus

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the **functional interaction** between the physical components of the Reynolds Experiment setup. It traces the path of water (flow) and the path of dye (tracer) through the apparatus.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Tank1[Overhead Constant Head Tank] -->|gravity feed| Valve1[Inlet Flow Control Valve]
    Valve1 -->|calmed by baffles| Inlet[Inlet Bell Mouth]
    Inlet -->|steady uniform entry| TestTube[Transparent Glass Test Tube of Diameter D]
    DyeRes[Beaker with Dye Solution] -->|hypodermic needle injection| TestTube
    TestTube -->|downstream observation zone| ObsWindow[Dye Observation Window]
    ObsWindow -->|flow exits| OutValve[Outlet Flow Control Valve]
    OutValve -->|measured discharge| CollectJar[Volumetric Collecting Jar]
    CollectJar -->|volume + time| StopWatch[Stop Watch for Time t]
    CollectJar -->|final Q| CalcUnit[Calculation of Vc and Re]
    ObsWindow -->|visual confirmation of regime| CalcUnit
    CalcUnit -->|result table| Report[Lab Report with Vc1 and Vc2]
```

### 4.1.1 Reading the Diagram — Functional Roles of Each Block

| Block | Functional Role |
|---|---|
| **Overhead Constant Head Tank** | Maintains a steady hydrostatic pressure so that inlet flow does not fluctuate during a reading |
| **Inlet Flow Control Valve** | Coarse adjustment of the volumetric flow rate entering the test tube |
| **Inlet Bell Mouth** | Smooths the entry profile to ensure fully-developed flow is reached inside the test section |
| **Transparent Glass Test Tube** | The "laboratory" — diameter $D$ is fixed and known for all calculations |
| **Beaker with Dye Solution** | Reservoir of the tracer (KMnO₄ or methylene blue) |
| **Dye Observation Window** | The section where the experimenter's eye is fixed to detect filament oscillation |
| **Outlet Flow Control Valve** | Fine adjustment — the primary knob used to gradually reduce velocity |
| **Volumetric Collecting Jar** | Captures water for a timed interval to compute experimental $Q$ |
| **Calculation Node** | Combines $Q$, $A$, and $\nu$ to produce $V_c$ and $Re$ |

## 4.2 Flow-Regime Decision Tree — Block Diagram

The following diagram is the **logical decision tree** that the student follows in the lab to identify which flow regime currently exists inside the tube.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([Start: Inject dye and set flow]) --> Obs1{Is dye filament perfectly straight and steady?}
    Obs1 -- Yes --> Lam[Confirmed LAMINAR Flow]
    Obs1 -- No --> Obs2{Is dye oscillating in a wave shape?}
    Obs2 -- Yes --> Trans[Confirmed TRANSITIONAL Flow]
    Obs2 -- No --> Obs3{Is dye fully mixed and uniformly coloured?}
    Obs3 -- Yes --> Turb[Confirmed TURBULENT Flow]
    Obs3 -- No --> Repeat[Wait and re-observe carefully]
    Repeat --> Obs1
    Lam --> Read1[Record Q at this V]
    Trans --> Read2[Note Q at boundary]
    Turb --> Read3[Reduce Q slowly to find boundary]
    Read1 --> Calc1[Compute Re from Q]
    Read2 --> Calc2[Compute Re from Q]
    Read3 --> Calc1
    Calc1 --> Result[Compare Re with 2000 and 4000]
    Calc2 --> Result
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Viva Tip:** When asked "Why do you need a constant head tank?" the correct answer is: "To eliminate the inertia and surface-wave fluctuations of a free-falling supply, so that the velocity entering the test tube is steady in time, which is a precondition for Reynolds' steady-state observation of flow regime."
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Define Reynolds Number. What is its physical significance?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Reynolds Number is a dimensionless number that represents the ratio of inertial forces to viscous forces acting on a fluid element in flow. It is given by $Re = \dfrac{\rho V D}{\mu} = \dfrac{V D}{\nu}$.

**Physical Significance:**
- When $Re$ is small, viscous forces dominate → the flow is **laminar** (smooth, layered).
- When $Re$ is large, inertial forces dominate → the flow is **turbulent** (chaotic, mixing).
- The numerical value of $Re$ (via the standard critical values 2000 and 4000) allows the engineer to **predict the flow regime** without visual observation, simply from operating parameters.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Differentiate between the lower and upper critical velocities.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Lower Critical Velocity ($V_{c1}$) | Upper Critical Velocity ($V_{c2}$) |
|---|---|---|
| Reynolds Number | 2000 | 4000 |
| Observation method | Dye oscillation disappears when reducing flow | Dye oscillation appears when increasing flow |
| Engineering use | Conservative safe limit below which flow is GUARANTEED laminar | Limit above which flow is GUARANTEED turbulent |
| Practical importance | **High** — used in design | Lower — mainly academic |
| Value of $V_c$ | $\dfrac{2000\nu}{D}$ | $\dfrac{4000\nu}{D}$ |

The gap $V_{c1}$ to $V_{c2}$ is the **transitional zone**, where the flow regime is unstable and depends on disturbances.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A [14 Marks] [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, describe the **experimental setup and procedure** to determine the **lower critical velocity** in pipe flow using the Reynolds apparatus. Mention the observations and the formula used. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** In a Reynolds apparatus, the test tube has an internal diameter of $D = 30$ mm. Water at 25 °C ($\nu = 0.89 \times 10^{-6}$ m²/s) flows through it. At the instant the dye filament just becomes steady, the volume of water collected in 20 seconds is 480 mL. Calculate the **experimental lower critical Reynolds number** and compare it with the standard value. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Solution to Question A

##### Part (a) — Apparatus, Procedure, and Formula (7 marks)

**Apparatus Used:**
- Overhead constant-head water tank with overflow arrangement
- Transparent glass test tube (typical bore 20–30 mm, length ~1.5 m) mounted horizontally
- Inlet bell mouth and calming section
- Dye reservoir (potassium permanganate solution) with a fine hypodermic injection needle
- Inlet and outlet flow-control valves
- Measuring cylinder (500 mL) and stopwatch

**Procedure:**

1. Fill the overhead tank and allow water to overflow so that a constant head is established.
2. Open the inlet valve and let water flow through the glass tube at a moderately high rate.
3. Allow a thin stream of dye to enter the test tube through the needle at the centreline of the inlet. At high flow, the dye mixes rapidly with water and appears as a **diffuse coloured cloud** — this indicates turbulent flow.
4. **Gradually reduce** the flow by slowly closing the outlet valve. Watch the dye behaviour through the observation window.
5. As flow decreases, the dye cloud begins to **concentrate** into a wavy, oscillating thread — this is the **transitional zone**.
6. Continue reducing the flow slowly. The dye oscillation amplitude decreases.
7. The moment the dye filament becomes a **perfectly straight, steady, unbroken thread** running the entire length of the tube, the flow has just become **laminar**. This is the **lower critical velocity** point.
8. **Without disturbing the flow**, place a measuring jar at the outlet and collect water for a measured time $t$.
9. Record the volume $V_o$, the time $t$, and the water temperature.
10. Calculate:
    - $Q = \dfrac{V_o}{t}$ (experimental discharge)
    - $A = \dfrac{\pi D^2}{4}$ (cross-sectional area of the tube)
    - $V_{c1} = \dfrac{Q}{A}$ (lower critical velocity)
    - $Re_{c1,\ \text{exp}} = \dfrac{V_{c1} \cdot D}{\nu}$ (experimental critical Reynolds number)
11. Compare $Re_{c1,\ \text{exp}}$ with the standard value of **2000**.

**Formula Used:**

$$Re = \frac{V D}{\nu}$$

**Observation Table (typical format):**

| Trial | Volume $V_o$ (mL) | Time $t$ (s) | $Q$ (m³/s) | $V_{c1}$ (m/s) | $Re_{c1,\ \text{exp}}$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 480 | 20 | … | … | … |
| 2 | 460 | 20 | … | … | … |
| 3 | 490 | 20 | … | … | … |

---

##### Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks)

**Given:**
- $D = 30$ mm $= 0.030$ m
- $\nu = 0.89 \times 10^{-6}$ m²/s
- $V_o = 480$ mL $= 480 \times 10^{-6}$ m³
- $t = 20$ s

**Step 1 — Compute Experimental Discharge**

$$
Q = \frac{V_o}{t} = \frac{480 \times 10^{-6}}{20} = 24 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^3/\text{s} = 2.4 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute Cross-Sectional Area**

$$
A = \frac{\pi D^2}{4} = \frac{\pi \times (0.030)^2}{4} = \frac{\pi \times 9 \times 10^{-4}}{4} = 7.0686 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2
$$

**Step 3 — Compute Lower Critical Velocity**

$$
V_{c1} = \frac{Q}{A} = \frac{2.4 \times 10^{-5}}{7.0686 \times 10^{-4}} = 0.03395 \text{ m/s}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute Experimental Lower Critical Reynolds Number**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Re_{c1,\ \text{exp}} &= \frac{V_{c1} \cdot D}{\nu} \\
&= \frac{0.03395 \times 0.030}{0.89 \times 10^{-6}} \\
&= \frac{1.0185 \times 10^{-3}}{0.89 \times 10^{-6}} \\
&= 1144.4
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Comparison with Standard Value**

$$
\text{Standard } Re_{c1} = 2000
$$

$$
\text{Experimental } Re_{c1} = 1144
$$

$$
\text{Percentage Error} = \frac{\vert 1144 - 2000 \vert}{2000} \times 100 = 42.8 \%
$$

**Inference (1 mark):**
The experimental value is **significantly lower** than the standard 2000. This is because:
- The observation of the "first straight thread" is highly subjective and depends on the experimenter's visual acuity and the dye concentration.
- The water temperature might have been higher than 25 °C, lowering $\nu$ further.
- The inlet conditions may not have been fully developed, giving a falsely low velocity at the observation point.
- Friction losses in the supply pipe cause the actual velocity in the test section to be lower than that calculated from $Q$.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** In 7-mark numerical sub-parts, you must show **all** intermediate steps. Skipping from $Q$ directly to $Re$ without the explicit $V = Q/A$ step costs you the **methodology mark** (1 mark) and frequently the **unit-consistency mark** (1 mark). Always write down the formula before substituting numbers.

---

### Question B [14 Marks — Alternative Choice] [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**(a)** Define **Critical Velocity**, **Lower Critical Velocity**, and **Upper Critical Velocity**. State the corresponding Reynolds number values and clearly explain the **three flow regimes** with a labelled sketch of the dye appearance in each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Crude oil of kinematic viscosity $\nu = 6.5 \times 10^{-5}$ m²/s is to be pumped through a 100 mm diameter horizontal pipe. If the maximum expected flow velocity is 2.0 m/s, determine the **flow regime** of the oil. Comment on whether the regime is acceptable for steady operation. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Solution to Question B

##### Part (a) — Definitions and Flow Regimes (7 marks)

**Definitions:**

- **Critical Velocity ($V_c$):** The mean velocity of fluid flow at which the flow regime transitions from laminar to turbulent. It corresponds to a specific Critical Reynolds Number $Re_c$.

- **Lower Critical Velocity ($V_{c1}$):** The velocity below which the flow is *guaranteed* to be laminar, regardless of the inlet disturbances. Corresponding to $Re_{c1} = 2000$.
  $$V_{c1} = \frac{2000 \cdot \nu}{D}$$

- **Upper Critical Velocity ($V_{c2}$):** The velocity above which the flow is *guaranteed* to be turbulent. Corresponding to $Re_{c2} = 4000$.
  $$V_{c2} = \frac{4000 \cdot \nu}{D}$$

**Three Flow Regimes:**

| Regime | Reynolds Number Range | Dye Appearance | Velocity Profile |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Laminar (Streamline)** | $Re < 2000$ | Straight, thin, unbroken coloured thread | Parabolic (max at centre, zero at wall) |
| **Transitional** | $2000 \le Re \le 4000$ | Dye oscillates in a wave; intermittent mixing | Unstable — fluctuates between profiles |
| **Turbulent** | $Re > 4000$ | Dye breaks up; uniform diffuse colour | Nearly flat (plug-flow) profile |

**Labelled Sketch of Dye Appearance (text representation):**

```
LAMINAR   :  |||||||||||||||  (one straight thread)
TRANSITIONAL:  ~~~~~~~~∿∿∿~~~  (oscillating wave)
TURBULENT :  ≈≈≈≈≈≈≈≈≈≈≈≈≈≈  (fully mixed, no thread)
```

**Inference:**
- For design, engineers usually enforce $V < V_{c1}$ for laminar operation.
- If turbulent flow is desired (e.g., for mixing), they enforce $V > V_{c2}$.

---

##### Part (b) — Numerical on Crude Oil (7 marks)

**Given:**
- $\nu = 6.5 \times 10^{-5}$ m²/s
- $D = 100$ mm $= 0.100$ m
- $V = 2.0$ m/s

**Step 1 — Compute Reynolds Number**

$$
Re = \frac{V \cdot D}{\nu} = \frac{2.0 \times 0.100}{6.5 \times 10^{-5}} = \frac{0.200}{6.5 \times 10^{-5}} = 3076.9
$$

**Step 2 — Identify the Flow Regime**

$$
Re = 3076.9 \quad \Rightarrow \quad 2000 < Re < 4000
$$

Therefore, the flow is in the **Transitional Regime**.

**Step 3 — Compute the Boundary Velocities for Comment**

$$
V_{c1} = \frac{2000 \times 6.5 \times 10^{-5}}{0.100} = 1.30 \text{ m/s}
$$

$$
V_{c2} = \frac{4000 \times 6.5 \times 10^{-5}}{0.100} = 2.60 \text{ m/s}
$$

**Step 4 — Comment on Acceptability**

Since the operating velocity $V = 2.0$ m/s lies in the range $V_{c1} < V < V_{c2}$, the flow is **unstable and unpredictable**. The regime can flip between laminar and turbulent depending on minor upstream disturbances (valve closures, pump surges, temperature fluctuations).

**Engineering Recommendation:**
- For **steady, predictable** operation, either:
  - **Reduce** the velocity to below $V_{c1} = 1.30$ m/s (laminar, but lower throughput), or
  - **Increase** the velocity to above $V_{c2} = 2.60$ m/s (fully turbulent, higher friction loss but stable).
- The transitional regime is **not acceptable** for critical applications such as metering or heat transfer, where the friction factor and heat transfer coefficient are highly sensitive to flow regime.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Many students stop at "the flow is transitional" and write no further comment. KTU 2024 Scheme demands an **engineering recommendation** for full marks in 7-mark numericals. Always conclude with: *"This regime is unsuitable for [specific application]; recommend $V < V_{c1}$ for laminar OR $V > V_{c2}$ for turbulent."*

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU 2024 Scheme**

### Core Definitions
- ☐ **Reynolds Number** $Re = \rho V D / \mu = V D / \nu$ — ratio of inertial to viscous forces.
- ☐ **Critical Velocity** $V_c$ — velocity at which the flow regime changes.
- ☐ **Lower Critical Velocity** $V_{c1}$ corresponds to $Re = 2000$ (always laminar below this).
- ☐ **Upper Critical Velocity** $V_{c2}$ corresponds to $Re = 4000$ (always turbulent above this).
- ☐ **Transitional Zone** is $2000 \le Re \le 4000$.

### Experimental Know-How
- ☐ The Reynolds apparatus uses a **transparent glass test tube**, **dye injection needle**, and **constant-head tank**.
- ☐ The dye used is typically **potassium permanganate** or **methylene blue**.
- ☐ To find the **lower** critical velocity, **decrease** flow from turbulent until the dye thread is just straight.
- ☐ To find the **upper** critical velocity, **increase** flow from laminar until the dye thread just begins to oscillate.
- ☐ Compute $Q = V_o / t$, then $V_c = Q / A = 4Q / (\pi D^2)$.
- ☐ Cross-sectional area of pipe: $A = \pi D^2 / 4$.

### Numerical Traps to Avoid
- ☐ Always convert diameter from **mm to m** before substituting in SI formulas.
- ☐ Always convert volume from **mL to m³** (1 mL = $10^{-6}$ m³).
- ☐ Always convert time from **minutes to seconds** if needed.
- ☐ For water at 20 °C, $\nu \approx 1.0 \times 10^{-6}$ m²/s. For higher temperatures, $\nu$ decreases.
- ☐ Standard critical Reynolds numbers are **dimensionless** — never attach units to them.
- ☐ The two critical velocities differ by a **factor of 2** (since $V_c \propto Re_c$).

### Viva-Voce Quick-Fire Answers
- ☐ **Q: Why is the Reynolds Number dimensionless?**
  **A:** Because all its variables are combined such that M, L, and T cancel out, leaving a pure number.
- ☐ **Q: Why do we need a constant-head tank?**
  **A:** To ensure steady, time-independent inflow so that the flow regime is unambiguously observable.
- ☐ **Q: Can the critical Reynolds number ever be 2300?**
  **A:** In the transitional zone, intermediate values are observed, but 2000 and 4000 are the **standard engineering limits**.
- ☐ **Q: What happens to $V_c$ if pipe diameter is doubled?**
  **A:** $V_c$ is **halved**, because $V_c \propto 1/D$.
- ☐ **Q: What happens to $V_c$ if fluid is heated?**
  **A:** $V_c$ **decreases**, because $\nu$ decreases with temperature for water.

> [!TIP]
> **Final KTU Golden Rule:** Whenever you see a fluid-flow problem, your very first calculation should always be the **Reynolds Number**. Two minutes of $Re$ computation can save you from using the wrong formula for the rest of the 14-mark question.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Determination of Minor Losses in Pipe Flow
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCNSL307 - FLUID MECHANICS LAB > - **Module:** Module Experiment 2 > - **Topic:** Determination of Minor Losses in Pipe Flow # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition In a pipe flow system, the **total head loss** ($h_L$) experienced by a fluid as it travels between two sections is broadly classified into two categories: 1. **Major Losses ($h_f$)** — Frictional losses occurring uniformly along the straight length of the pipe, governed by the Darcy–Weisbach equation. 2. **Minor Losses ($h_m$)** — Localised head losses produced by **pipe fittings, valves, bends, elbows, sudden contractions, sudden expansions, and entries/exits** that disturb the smooth streamline flow. The **minor loss** for a particular fitting is mathematically expressed as: $$h_m \;=\; K \,\frac{V^{2}}{2g}$$ where $K$ is the dimensionless **loss coefficient** of the fitting, $V$ is the **mean velocity of flow** in the pipe (in m/s), and $g$ is the acceleration due to gravity (**9.81 m/s²**). > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Note:** Despite being called "minor," in short laboratory pipe setups with many fittings, minor losses can often **exceed major (frictional) losses**. The experiment aims to *experimentally determine* the loss coefficient $K$ for various fittings and compare it against standard textbook values. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Garden Hose Intuition Imagine you are **watering your garden** with a long hose fitted with a nozzle at the end. The water pressure drops along the hose (major loss due to friction along the walls). Now, suppose you add a **sharp 90° bend** in the hose, or you suddenly squeeze the pipe to half its diameter, or you attach a **gate valve** and partly close it. - Each of these fittings causes an **extra, localised pressure drop** — even though the pipe length did not change. - The sharper the bend, the tighter the valve, the more turbulent the flow becomes at that point, and the **greater the energy wasted** as eddies and sound. > **Think of minor losses as the "speed-breakers" of fluid flow.** The flow has to "jump" over each fitting, losing a chunk of its kinetic energy in the process. The loss coefficient $K$ is simply a numerical measure of how tall that speed-breaker is for that particular fitting. ## 1.3 Standard Fittings Studied in the KTU Lab | S.No | Fitting | Typical Source of Loss | |------|---------|------------------------| | 1 | Sudden Contraction | Flow converges abruptly, creating jet contraction and eddies | | 2 | Sudden Expansion | Flow separates from the wall, forming a turbulent mixing zone | | 3 | 90° Elbow (Long Radius) | Centrifugal effect + secondary circulation | | 4 | 90° Elbow (Short Radius) | Sharper curvature → higher curvature loss | | 5 | Globe Valve (Fully Open) | Flow path zig-zags through seat | | 6 | Gate Valve (Fully Open) | Flow passes under the gate with minimal obstruction | | 7 | Pipe Entry (Re-entrant / Sharp) | Jet contraction at entry | > [!NOTE] > In the KTU PCNSL307 lab, the experiment is typically conducted on a **Minor Losses Test Rig** where the test fitting can be interchanged, and the discharge is regulated to obtain a range of flow rates. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Theoretical Background When fluid flows through a fitting, the streamline pattern is **disturbed** within a short length and the flow separates, creating **eddies and turbulence**. This dissipation of mechanical energy appears as a loss of pressure head between two sections located just upstream and just downstream of the fitting. ### 2.1.1 Components of Head Loss in a Pipe System $$h_{\text{total}} \;=\; h_f \;+\; \sum h_m$$ where $$h_f \;=\; \frac{4 f L V^{2}}{2 g D} \quad \text{(Darcy–Weisbach major loss)}$$ $$h_m \;=\; K \,\frac{V^{2}}{2g} \quad \text{(Minor loss for a single fitting)}$$ ### 2.1.2 Why "Minor" Losses Are Often NOT Minor The designation "minor" is historical, not physical. For a system with $n$ fittings, the equivalent length concept reveals: $$h_m \;=\; \frac{4 f L_e V^{2}}{2 g D} \quad\Rightarrow\quad L_e \;=\; \frac{K D}{4 f}$$ If $f \approx 0.02$ and $K = 10$ (typical for a globe valve), then $L_e = 125 \,D$. In a 25 mm diameter pipe, that is **over 3 metres of equivalent straight pipe** for a *single valve*. ## 2.2 Loss Coefficients for Standard Fittings (Standard Values) | Fitting | Loss Coefficient $K$ | Remarks | |---------|----------------------|---------| | Sudden Expansion (Area ratio $A_1/A_2$) | $K = \left(1 - \dfrac{A_1}{A_2}\right)^{2}$ | Theoretical (Borda–Carnot) | | Sudden Contraction | $K = 0.5\left(1 - \dfrac{A_2}{A_1}\right)$ | Approximate, based on $V_2$ | | 90° Long-Radius Elbow | $0.2$ to $0.3$ | — | | 90° Short-Radius Elbow | $0.9$ to $1.0$ | — | | 45° Elbow | $0.3$ to $0.4$ | — | | Globe Valve (Fully Open) | $10.0$ | Highest in typical list | | Gate Valve (Fully Open) | $0.2$ | Lowest when fully open | | Re-entrant (Sharp) Entry | $0.8$ to $1.0$ | — | | Sharp-Edged Exit | $1.0$ | Full velocity head lost | > [!NOTE] > These $K$ values are referenced against the **velocity in the smaller (or downstream) pipe section**, except for sudden expansion where $K$ is referenced against the **upstream velocity** $V_1$ in some conventions. Always check the convention in your lab manual. ## 2.3 The Theoretical Derivation Logic for Sudden Expansion (Borda–Carnot Loss) The most important theoretical result the examiner expects is the **Borda–Carnot equation** for sudden expansion: $$h_{L,\text{exp}} \;=\; \frac{\left(V_{1} - V_{2}\right)^{2}}{2g} \;=\; \left(1 - \frac{A_1}{A_2}\right)^{2} \frac{V_{1}^{2}}{2g}$$ > **Physical Intuition:** When flow suddenly expands, the high-velocity jet from the smaller pipe cannot "fill" the larger pipe immediately. A **turbulent eddying zone** forms in the corners where the flow separates. The kinetic energy of this jet is gradually dissipated as it mixes with the slower surrounding fluid — and this dissipation **is the head loss**. ## 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (Exam-Ready) | # | Formula | Description | Units | |---|---------|-------------|-------| | 1 | $h_m = K \cdot \dfrac{V^{2}}{2g}$ | Minor loss via loss coefficient | m of fluid | | 2 | $h_m = \dfrac{4 f L_e V^{2}}{2 g D}$ | Minor loss via equivalent length | m of fluid | | 3 | $L_e = \dfrac{K D}{4 f}$ | Equivalent length of fitting | m | | 4 | $V = \dfrac{Q}{A} = \dfrac{4Q}{\pi D^{2}}$ | Mean velocity in pipe | m/s | | 5 | $K = \dfrac{h_m \cdot 2g}{V^{2}}$ | Experimental loss coefficient (key result) | dimensionless | | 6 | $h_m = \dfrac{\Delta p}{\rho g}$ | Head loss from pressure tappings (gauge) | m | | 7 | $h_m = h \left(\dfrac{\rho_m}{\rho_w} - 1\right)$ | Head loss from U-tube differential manometer (mercury) | m of water | | 8 | $K_{\text{exp}} = \left(1 - \dfrac{A_1}{A_2}\right)^{2}$ | Theoretical $K$ for sudden expansion | dimensionless | | 9 | $Q = \dfrac{A_c \cdot H}{t}$ | Discharge from collecting tank (volume method) | m³/s | | 10 | $Re = \dfrac{\rho V D}{\mu} = \dfrac{V D}{\nu}$ | Reynolds number for flow regime check | dimensionless | ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility | Field | Application | |-------|-------------| | **Water Supply Networks** | Sizing pumps by accounting for losses in valves and bends | | **HVAC Systems** | Designing ductwork with minimum pressure drop across fittings | | **Oil & Gas Pipelines** | Calculating pumping station spacing and energy cost | | **Fire-Fighting Hydrant Networks** | Ensuring adequate pressure at the hydrant nozzle | | **Power Plant Cooling Systems** | Optimising condenser water flow with minimum pumping cost | | **Chemical Process Plants** | Designing piping for minimum residence time and energy efficiency | # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of the Minor Loss Equation from Energy Balance Consider two sections, **①** (just upstream of the fitting) and **②** (just downstream, where flow has re-stabilised). Applying the **modified Bernoulli equation** (energy equation with loss) between these sections: $$\frac{p_{1}}{\rho g} + \frac{V_{1}^{2}}{2g} + z_{1} \;=\; \frac{p_{2}}{\rho g} + \frac{V_{2}^{2}}{2g} + z_{2} + h_m$$ For a horizontal test section, $z_{1} = z_{2}$. The minor loss $h_m$ is therefore the **difference in piezometric + kinetic head** between the two sections: $$h_m \;=\; \left(\frac{p_{1}}{\rho g} + \frac{V_{1}^{2}}{2g}\right) - \left(\frac{p_{2}}{\rho g} + \frac{V_{2}^{2}}{2g}\right)$$ Rearranging, the loss is defined in terms of the **kinetic energy term** through the dimensionless coefficient $K$: $$h_m \;\equiv\; K \cdot \frac{V_{\text{ref}}^{2}}{2g}$$ where $V_{\text{ref}}$ is the reference velocity (typically the **downstream pipe velocity** $V_2$ for contractions and elbows, and $V_1$ for sudden expansion when applying the Borda–Carnot form). --- ## 3.2 Derivation of the Borda–Carnot Loss (Sudden Expansion) Consider a pipe carrying fluid from a small section of area $A_1$ (and velocity $V_1$) into a larger section of area $A_2$ (and velocity $V_2$). **Step 1 — Continuity Equation:** $$A_1 V_1 \;=\; A_2 V_2 \quad\Rightarrow\quad V_2 \;=\; V_1 \cdot \frac{A_1}{A_2}$$ **Step 2 — Momentum Equation (Control Volume Approach):** Apply the linear momentum theorem to a control volume enclosing the expansion. The net pressure force equals the rate of change of momentum: $$p_1 A_2 - p_2 A_2 \;=\; \rho Q \left(V_2 - V_1\right)$$ where $Q = A_1 V_1 = A_2 V_2$. Substituting $Q$: $$p_1 A_2 - p_2 A_2 \;=\; \rho A_2 V_2 \left(V_2 - V_1\right)$$ **Step 3 — Simplify by Dividing Both Sides by $A_2$:** $$p_1 - p_2 \;=\; \rho V_2 \left(V_2 - V_1\right)$$ **Step 4 — Express the Head Form (Divide by $\rho g$):** $$\frac{p_1 - p_2}{\rho g} \;=\; \frac{V_2 \left(V_2 - V_1\right)}{g}$$ **Step 5 — Use Energy Equation for a Horizontal Pipe (No Change in Elevation):** $$\frac{p_1}{\rho g} + \frac{V_1^{2}}{2g} \;=\; \frac{p_2}{\rho g} + \frac{V_2^{2}}{2g} + h_L$$ Solving for the head loss: $$h_L \;=\; \frac{p_1 - p_2}{\rho g} + \frac{V_1^{2} - V_2^{2}}{2g}$$ **Step 6 — Substitute the Momentum Result into the Energy Equation:** $$h_L \;=\; \frac{V_2 \left(V_2 - V_1\right)}{g} + \frac{V_1^{2} - V_2^{2}}{2g}$$ Take the common denominator $2g$: $$h_L \;=\; \frac{2 V_2 \left(V_2 - V_1\right) + V_1^{2} - V_2^{2}}{2g}$$ **Step 7 — Expand the Numerator:** $$2 V_2^{2} - 2 V_1 V_2 + V_1^{2} - V_2^{2} \;=\; V_2^{2} - 2 V_1 V_2 + V_1^{2} \;=\; \left(V_1 - V_2\right)^{2}$$ **Step 8 — Final Borda–Carnot Result:** $$\boxed{\,h_{L,\text{exp}} \;=\; \frac{\left(V_1 - V_2\right)^{2}}{2g}\,}$$ **Step 9 — Express in terms of $K$ and $V_1$ (using $V_2 = V_1 A_1 / A_2$):** $$V_1 - V_2 \;=\; V_1 - V_1 \frac{A_1}{A_2} \;=\; V_1 \left(1 - \frac{A_1}{A_2}\right)$$ Therefore: $$h_{L,\text{exp}} \;=\; \left(1 - \frac{A_1}{A_2}\right)^{2} \frac{V_1^{2}}{2g} \quad\Rightarrow\quad \boxed{\,K_{\text{exp}} \;=\; \left(1 - \frac{A_1}{A_2}\right)^{2}\,}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Conventions differ between textbooks:** Some define $K$ with respect to $V_1$ (as above for expansion), some with respect to $V_2$. In the KTU lab manual, the convention is usually $V$ = velocity in the **smaller pipe (upstream)** for expansion and the **downstream pipe** for contraction. Always state the convention clearly in your answer script. --- ## 3.3 Experimental Calculation Procedure — Analytical Matrix The lab experiment follows a systematic procedure. The table below summarises the **step-by-step analytical matrix** that converts raw observations into the final loss coefficient $K$. | Step | Observation / Measurement | Formula / Conversion | Result | |------|--------------------------|----------------------|--------| | 1 | Measure rise in collecting tank $H$ in time $t$ | $V_{\text{water}} = A_c \cdot H$ | Volume in m³ | | 2 | Calculate discharge | $Q = \dfrac{A_c \cdot H}{t}$ | m³/s | | 3 | Measure pipe diameter $D$ | $A = \dfrac{\pi D^{2}}{4}$ | m² | | 4 | Calculate mean velocity | $V = \dfrac{Q}{A}$ | m/s | | 5 | Read manometer levels $h_1$ and $h_2$ | $h = \vert h_1 - h_2 \vert$ | m of mercury | | 6 | Convert manometer reading to head of water | $h_m = h \left(\dfrac{\rho_{\text{Hg}}}{\rho_w} - 1\right) = 12.6 \, h$ | m of water | | 7 | Compute experimental $K$ | $K_{\text{exp}} = \dfrac{h_m \cdot 2g}{V^{2}}$ | dimensionless | | 8 | Compare with standard $K$ | $\%\text{ Error} = \dfrac{\vert K_{\text{std}} - K_{\text{exp}} \vert}{K_{\text{std}}} \times 100$ | % | | 9 | Plot $h_m$ vs $V^{2}$ | Linear fit slope $m = \dfrac{K}{2g}$ | Verify linearity | | 10 | Calculate Reynolds number | $Re = \dfrac{V D}{\nu}$ | Check flow regime | > [!NOTE] > For water at room temperature, $\nu \approx 1.0 \times 10^{-6}$ m²/s. The Reynolds number is often calculated and reported in the lab report to **justify that the flow is turbulent** ($Re > 4000$), which is the operating range where most published $K$ values are valid. ## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example **Problem:** Water flows through a 50 mm diameter horizontal pipe fitted with a **90° short-radius elbow**. The discharge is measured as $Q = 2.5$ litres/s. A differential mercury manometer connected across the elbow (with water as the manometric fluid below mercury) shows a deflection of $h = 30$ mm. Determine the experimental loss coefficient $K$ of the elbow. **Given Data:** - $D = 50$ mm $= 0.05$ m - $Q = 2.5 \times 10^{-3}$ m³/s - $h = 0.030$ m (mercury deflection) - $g = 9.81$ m/s² - $\rho_{\text{Hg}} = 13600$ kg/m³, $\rho_w = 1000$ kg/m³ **Step 1 — Pipe Cross-Sectional Area:** $$A \;=\; \frac{\pi (0.05)^{2}}{4} \;=\; 1.9635 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^{2}$$ **Step 2 — Mean Velocity:** $$V \;=\; \frac{Q}{A} \;=\; \frac{2.5 \times 10^{-3}}{1.9635 \times 10^{-3}} \;=\; 1.273 \text{ m/s}$$ **Step 3 — Manometer Equation (Head of Water Equivalent):** $$h_m \;=\; h \left(\frac{\rho_{\text{Hg}}}{\rho_w} - 1\right) \;=\; 0.030 \times \left(13.6 - 1\right) \;=\; 0.030 \times 12.6 \;=\; 0.378 \text{ m of water}$$ **Step 4 — Experimental Loss Coefficient:** $$K_{\text{exp}} \;=\; \frac{h_m \cdot 2g}{V^{2}} \;=\; \frac{0.378 \times 2 \times 9.81}{(1.273)^{2}} \;=\; \frac{7.416}{1.6205} \;=\; 4.576$$ **Step 5 — Comparison with Standard:** Standard value for 90° short-radius elbow is typically $K \approx 0.9$. The experimental result of $4.58$ is significantly higher, indicating possible issues such as: - Air entrainment in manometer lines - Calibration error in $h$ reading - Pipe roughness higher than expected - Non-uniform velocity profile at the tapping points # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Typical Minor Losses Test Rig ```mermaid flowchart LR A1[Constant Head
Overhead Tank] --> B1[Control Valve
for Flow Regulation] B1 --> C1[Straight Inlet Pipe
with Stilling Section] C1 --> D1[Test Fitting
Sudden Contraction] D1 --> E1[Pressure Tapping 1
Upstream Manometer Limb] D1 --> E2[Pressure Tapping 2
Downstream Manometer Limb] E1 --> F1[Differential U-tube
Mercury Manometer] E2 --> F1 D1 --> G1[Straight Outlet Pipe] G1 --> H1[Measuring Tank
with Stopwatch] H1 --> I1[Volumetric Discharge
Calculation Block] F1 --> J1[Head Loss Calculation
and K Determination] I1 --> J1 J1 --> K1[Result Table
K vs Standard Value] classDef tank fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#1e40af,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000 classDef valve fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#b45309,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000 classDef pipe fill:#e0e7ff,stroke:#3730a3,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000 classDef test fill:#fce7f3,stroke:#9d174d,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000 classDef meter fill:#dcfce7,stroke:#166534,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000 classDef compute fill:#f3e8ff,stroke:#6b21a8,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000 class A1,H1 tank class B1 valve class C1,G1 pipe class D1 test class E1,E2,F1 meter class I1,J1,K1 compute ``` ## 4.2 Schematic of Flow Disturbance and Energy Dissipation Across a Fitting ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph INLET[Upstream Section ①] A2[Smooth Streamline Flow
Piezometric Head p1 by rho g
Velocity V1] end subgraph FITTING[Test Fitting Region] B2[Flow Separation
Eddy Formation Zone
Turbulent Mixing] end subgraph OUTLET[Downstream Section ②] C2[Re-established Flow
Piezometric Head p2 by rho g
Velocity V2] end A2 --> B2 B2 --> C2 B2 -.Dissipated Energy.-> D2[Lost Head hm
hm = K times V squared over 2g] classDef sec fill:#f1f5f9,stroke:#0f172a,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000 classDef loss fill:#fee2e2,stroke:#991b1b,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000 class A2,B2,C2 sec class D2 loss ``` ## 4.3 Interchangeable Test Fittings — Modular Test Section ```mermaid flowchart TB P1[Inlet Flange] --> P2[Quick-Release
Coupling] P2 --> P3{Test Fitting
Selector Block} P3 --> P4a[Sudden
Contraction] P3 --> P4b[Sudden
Expansion] P3 --> P4c[90 Degree
Long Elbow] P3 --> P4d[90 Degree
Short Elbow] P3 --> P4e[Globe
Valve] P3 --> P4f[Gate
Valve] P4a --> P5[Outlet Flange] P4b --> P5 P4c --> P5 P4d --> P5 P4e --> P5 P4f --> P5 P5 --> P6[Outlet to
Measuring Tank] classDef flow fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#1e3a8a,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000 classDef choice fill:#fef9c3,stroke:#854d0e,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000 classDef fitting fill:#fce7f3,stroke:#9d174d,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000 class P1,P2,P5,P6 flow class P3 choice class P4a,P4b,P4c,P4d,P4e,P4f fitting ``` > [!NOTE] > **Operational Note:** Each fitting is mounted between two flanges with pressure tappings on either side. The tappings connect to a common differential manometer. The test fitting is changed, the flow is stabilised, and the readings are repeated for the new fitting. Multiple flow rates (typically 4–5) are used to plot $h_m$ vs $V^2$ and verify that the slope equals $K / (2g)$. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Question]** **Q1. Define minor losses in a pipe flow system. Why are they called "minor" even when they can dominate the total loss in a pipeline?** **Model Answer:** Minor losses are the localised head losses that occur in a pipe flow system when the fluid passes through fittings such as bends, elbows, valves, sudden contractions, and sudden expansions. They arise due to the **separation of flow from the boundary, formation of eddies, and dissipation of kinetic energy** into heat and sound in the immediate vicinity of the fitting. Mathematically, each minor loss is expressed as: $$h_m = K \cdot \frac{V^{2}}{2g}$$ These are called "minor" because, in **long pipelines**, the frictional (major) loss along the straight length is usually the dominant component. However, in **short pipe systems with many fittings** (such as lab setups, hydraulic machinery installations, and HVAC ducts), the cumulative minor losses can **exceed the major losses** and must not be neglected. > **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Question]** **Q2. State the Borda–Carnot equation for head loss due to sudden expansion. What is the corresponding loss coefficient $K$?** **Model Answer:** The Borda–Carnot equation for the head loss due to sudden expansion of a pipe from area $A_1$ (upstream) to area $A_2$ (downstream) is: $$h_{L,\text{exp}} = \frac{\left(V_1 - V_2\right)^{2}}{2g}$$ Using continuity $V_2 = V_1 \cdot A_1 / A_2$, this can be rewritten in terms of the upstream velocity $V_1$ as: $$h_{L,\text{exp}} = \left(1 - \frac{A_1}{A_2}\right)^{2} \frac{V_1^{2}}{2g}$$ Therefore, the loss coefficient for sudden expansion is: $$K_{\text{exp}} = \left(1 - \frac{A_1}{A_2}\right)^{2}$$ This is a **theoretical** result derived from a combined application of the continuity, momentum, and energy equations to a control volume enclosing the expansion. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice) > **[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Question]** ### Question A (14 Marks) **(a)** Derive an expression for the head loss due to **sudden expansion** of a pipe using the continuity, momentum, and energy equations. Clearly state the assumptions made during the derivation. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** In a pipe of diameter $D_1 = 75$ mm carrying water, a sudden expansion to $D_2 = 150$ mm occurs. The discharge through the system is $Q = 6$ litres/s. Calculate: (i) The head loss due to sudden expansion. (ii) The experimental loss coefficient $K$ (based on $V_1$). (iii) The equivalent length of the expansion in terms of pipe diameter $D_1$, assuming Darcy friction factor $f = 0.025$. **(7 Marks)** --- **Model Solution for Question A:** ### Part (a) — Derivation **Assumptions:** 1. The pipe is horizontal ($z_1 = z_2$). 2. The flow is steady, incompressible, and along the pipe axis. 3. The pressure at sections ① and ② is uniform across the cross-section. 4. No external work is done and no friction acts on the control volume walls (friction is lumped into $h_L$). **Step 1 — Continuity:** $A_1 V_1 = A_2 V_2$ **Step 2 — Momentum Equation** (control volume enclosing the expansion): $$p_1 A_2 - p_2 A_2 = \rho Q (V_2 - V_1) = \rho A_2 V_2 (V_2 - V_1)$$ **Step 3 — Energy Equation** (Bernoulli with loss): $$\frac{p_1}{\rho g} + \frac{V_1^2}{2g} = \frac{p_2}{\rho g} + \frac{V_2^2}{2g} + h_L$$ **Step 4 — Combine:** Substituting the momentum result and simplifying (as derived in Section 3.2) gives the Borda–Carnot result: $$\boxed{\,h_L = \frac{(V_1 - V_2)^2}{2g} = \left(1 - \frac{A_1}{A_2}\right)^2 \frac{V_1^2}{2g}\,}$$ ### Part (b) — Numerical Solution **Given:** $D_1 = 0.075$ m, $D_2 = 0.150$ m, $Q = 6 \times 10^{-3}$ m³/s, $f = 0.025$, $g = 9.81$ m/s². **(i) Head Loss:** Areas: $$A_1 = \frac{\pi (0.075)^2}{4} = 4.418 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$ $$A_2 = \frac{\pi (0.150)^2}{4} = 1.767 \times 10^{-2} \text{ m}^2$$ Velocities: $$V_1 = \frac{Q}{A_1} = \frac{6 \times 10^{-3}}{4.418 \times 10^{-3}} = 1.358 \text{ m/s}$$ $$V_2 = \frac{Q}{A_2} = \frac{6 \times 10^{-3}}{1.767 \times 10^{-2}} = 0.3395 \text{ m/s}$$ Head loss: $$h_L = \frac{(1.358 - 0.3395)^2}{2 \times 9.81} = \frac{(1.0185)^2}{19.62} = \frac{1.0374}{19.62} = 0.0529 \text{ m}$$ **(ii) Loss Coefficient:** $$K_{\text{exp}} = \left(1 - \frac{A_1}{A_2}\right)^2 = \left(1 - \frac{4.418 \times 10^{-3}}{1.767 \times 10^{-2}}\right)^2 = (1 - 0.25)^2 = (0.75)^2 = 0.5625$$ **(iii) Equivalent Length:** $$L_e = \frac{K D_1}{4 f} = \frac{0.5625 \times 0.075}{4 \times 0.025} = \frac{0.04219}{0.10} = 0.4219 \text{ m} = 42.2 \text{ cm}$$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Question A:** > 1. Students frequently **forget the area ratio** when reporting the loss coefficient and instead incorrectly use the diameter ratio squared. > 2. The $K$ value must be explicitly stated with the **reference velocity** convention (here $V_1$). > 3. In part (b)(iii), do not forget to multiply $K$ by $D_1$ (the **upstream diameter**, not the larger one). --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice > **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model Question]** **(a)** Describe with a neat sketch the **experimental setup** used to determine the minor losses in a pipe flow system. List the apparatus used and the procedure for conducting the experiment. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** During a minor losses experiment on a 40 mm diameter pipe fitted with a **globe valve (fully open)**, the following observations were recorded. A differential mercury manometer connected across the valve showed a deflection of $h = 45$ mm. The discharge was measured using a collecting tank of area $0.25$ m², and the rise in water level was 200 mm in 30 seconds. Determine: (i) The discharge through the pipe. (ii) The mean velocity in the pipe. (iii) The head loss across the valve in metres of water. (iv) The experimental loss coefficient $K$ for the valve. **(7 Marks)** --- **Model Solution for Question B:** ### Part (a) — Experimental Setup **Apparatus Required:** 1. **Constant-head overhead tank** (or sump with a centrifugal pump and a control valve to maintain steady flow). 2. **Test pipe section** of known diameter (e.g., 25–50 mm GI/MS pipe) fitted with a **quick-change fitting holder** so that the test fitting can be interchanged. 3. **Differential U-tube mercury manometer** connected to two pressure tappings located 5 to 10 pipe diameters upstream and downstream of the test fitting. 4. **Collecting tank** of known cross-sectional area with a **stopwatch** for volumetric measurement of discharge. 5. **Control valve** at the inlet to vary the flow rate. 6. **Measuring cylinder or hook gauge** to read the water level in the collecting tank. **Procedure:** 1. Set up the test section with the desired fitting (e.g., 90° elbow). 2. Open the inlet valve to a small flow rate and allow the system to **stabilise** (steady manometer reading). 3. Note the **manometer deflection** $h$ (difference in mercury levels in the two limbs). 4. Simultaneously, start the stopwatch and collect the water in the measuring tank for a fixed time $t$. 5. Measure the **rise in water level** $H$ in the collecting tank. 6. **Repeat** for 4–5 different flow rates by progressively opening the control valve. 7. **Replace** the test fitting with another (e.g., globe valve) and repeat steps 2–6. 8. For each trial, calculate $Q$, $V$, $h_m$, and $K_{\text{exp}}$. 9. Plot $h_m$ vs $V^2$. The slope of the straight line equals $K / (2g)$, from which $K$ can be obtained. 10. Compare $K_{\text{exp}}$ with the standard value and compute the percentage error. ### Part (b) — Numerical Solution **Given:** $D = 0.040$ m, $h = 0.045$ m, $A_c = 0.25$ m², $H = 0.200$ m, $t = 30$ s, $g = 9.81$ m/s². **(i) Discharge:** $$Q = \frac{A_c \cdot H}{t} = \frac{0.25 \times 0.200}{30} = \frac{0.05}{30} = 1.667 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^{3}/\text{s}$$ **(ii) Mean Velocity:** $$A = \frac{\pi (0.040)^2}{4} = 1.2566 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^{2}$$ $$V = \frac{Q}{A} = \frac{1.667 \times 10^{-3}}{1.2566 \times 10^{-3}} = 1.327 \text{ m/s}$$ **(iii) Head Loss Across the Valve (in metres of water):** $$h_m = h \left(\frac{\rho_{\text{Hg}}}{\rho_w} - 1\right) = 0.045 \times (13.6 - 1) = 0.045 \times 12.6 = 0.567 \text{ m of water}$$ **(iv) Experimental Loss Coefficient:** $$K_{\text{exp}} = \frac{h_m \cdot 2g}{V^{2}} = \frac{0.567 \times 2 \times 9.81}{(1.327)^{2}} = \frac{11.124}{1.761} = 6.32$$ **Comparison with standard:** Standard $K$ for a fully open globe valve is approximately **10.0**. The experimental value of **6.32** is lower, possibly indicating that the valve was not fully open or that the manometer tapping locations were too close to the valve (in the separated flow region, where static pressure is unrepresentative). > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Question B:** > 1. **Manometer reading** — Students often confuse the "manometer deflection" with the head loss directly. Remember, the deflection $h$ is in **mm of mercury**, and it must be converted to **head of water** using the factor $\left(\rho_{\text{Hg}}/\rho_w - 1\right) \approx 12.6$. > 2. **Discharge formula** — A common mistake is to use the **pipe area** instead of the **collecting tank area** when computing $Q$. Always use the tank area. > 3. **Units consistency** — Express all lengths in **metres** before substituting into equations. Mixing mm and m will lead to errors of orders of magnitude. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist** - ✅ **Minor losses** = localised head losses at fittings; given by $h_m = K \cdot \dfrac{V^2}{2g}$. - ✅ **Loss coefficient $K$** is a **dimensionless** number that quantifies the energy dissipation of a particular fitting. - ✅ **Borda–Carnot equation** for sudden expansion: $h_L = \dfrac{(V_1 - V_2)^2}{2g}$ — this is a **theoretical** result, not empirical. - ✅ Equivalent length concept: $L_e = \dfrac{K D}{4 f}$ — converts a minor loss into an equivalent length of straight pipe. - ✅ Manometer equation (mercury + water): $h_m = h \left(\dfrac{\rho_{\text{Hg}}}{\rho_w} - 1\right) = 12.6 \, h$ (when $h$ is in metres of mercury). - ✅ Discharge by volumetric method: $Q = \dfrac{A_c \cdot H}{t}$ — use **tank area**, not pipe area. - ✅ Velocity in pipe: $V = \dfrac{4Q}{\pi D^2}$ — always compute this **after** $Q$ and **before** $K$. - ✅ Experimental $K$ calculation: $K_{\text{exp}} = \dfrac{h_m \cdot 2g}{V^2}$ — this is the **final result** of the experiment. - ✅ **Plot** $h_m$ vs $V^2$ — must pass through the origin with slope $K/(2g)$; deviation from linearity indicates experimental error. - ✅ **Reynolds number check** — typical lab flows are turbulent ($Re > 4000$); reported $K$ values are valid in this regime. - ✅ **"Minor" is a misnomer** — in short pipe systems with multiple fittings, $\sum h_m$ can exceed $h_f$ significantly. - ✅ **Convention awareness** — always state whether $K$ is referenced to $V_1$ or $V_2$ (most lab manuals use downstream $V_2$ for contractions, upstream $V_1$ for expansion). - ✅ **Globe valve** has the **highest** $K$ (~10) among common fittings; **gate valve (fully open)** has the **lowest** (~0.2). - ✅ **Pressure tappings** must be located **at least 5–10 pipe diameters** away from the fitting to avoid being in the disturbed flow zone. - ✅ **Sources of error** in the lab: manometer parallax, air bubbles in manometer lines, unsteady flow, partially closed control valves upstream affecting flow profile. - ✅ **KTU 2024 scheme emphasis:** Expect derivation-based questions on Borda–Carnot, numerical problems on $K$ determination, and conceptual questions on the "minor" terminology. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Final Exam Tip for PCNSL307:** When writing your lab record, always include a **calibration table** for the manometer, a **graph** of $h_m$ vs $V^2$ (with linear regression line), and a **percentage error analysis** comparing $K_{\text{exp}}$ with the standard value. KTU evaluators consistently award higher marks to records that demonstrate **analytical depth** beyond the basic tabulation.
