> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCNSL307 - FLUID MECHANICS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Determination of Type of Flow Using Reynolds Number

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

The **Reynolds Number Experiment** is a classical fluid mechanics laboratory test designed to visually demonstrate and quantitatively classify the **regime of fluid flow** in a circular pipe. The dimensionless parameter used to classify the flow is the **Reynolds Number (Re)**, defined as the ratio of **inertial forces** to **viscous forces** acting within a fluid.

Mathematically, Reynolds Number is expressed as:

$$\text{Re} = \frac{\rho V D}{\mu} = \frac{V D}{\nu}$$

Where the symbols and their standard SI units are:
- $\rho$ = Mass density of the fluid $[\text{kg/m}^3]$
- $V$ = Mean (average) velocity of flow $[\text{m/s}]$
- $D$ = Internal diameter of the pipe $[\text{m}]$
- $\mu$ = Dynamic viscosity of the fluid $[\text{N·s/m}^2 \text{ or Pa·s}]$
- $\nu$ = Kinematic viscosity of the fluid $[\text{m}^2/\text{s}]$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Examiner Definition (Memorize This):**
> "Reynolds Number is a dimensionless quantity that predicts the nature of fluid flow — whether it will be **laminar**, **transitional**, or **turbulent** — by comparing the magnitude of inertial forces to viscous forces within the fluid."

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine you are standing at the edge of a calm, slow-moving river pouring honey into it. The honey oozes in smooth, parallel, undisturbed layers — this is **laminar flow**, where viscous forces dominate.

Now imagine the same river during a flood, rushing violently with eddies, swirls, and chaotic mixing — this is **turbulent flow**, where inertial forces dominate.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Traffic Analogy:**
> Think of cars on a highway as "fluid particles."
> - At **low speed** (laminar): Cars move in neat, organized lanes. Each car follows predictably.
> - At **high speed** (turbulent): Cars weave, overtake chaotically, and create unpredictable swirls.
>
> The **Reynolds Number** is the mathematical "speedometer" that tells you whether the fluid is behaving like the organized traffic (laminar) or the chaotic traffic (turbulent).

The **threshold value** where this transition occurs is known as the **Critical Reynolds Number**. For flow in a circular pipe:
- $\text{Re} < 2000$ → **Laminar Flow** (Streamline / orderly)
- $2000 < \text{Re} < 4000$ → **Transitional Flow** (Unstable, oscillating)
- $\text{Re} > 4000$ → **Turbulent Flow** (Chaotic, eddying)

## 1.3 The Osborne Reynolds Experiment (Historical Context)

In **1883**, Irish engineer **Osborne Reynolds** performed a pioneering experiment at the University of Manchester to visualize flow regimes. He injected a thin filament of **dye** into the center of a transparent glass pipe through which water flowed at varying rates.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Observation by Reynolds:**
> - At low flow rates, the **dye filament remained straight and intact**, indicating **laminar flow**.
> - At high flow rates, the **dye filament broke up and diffused** rapidly throughout the pipe, indicating **turbulent flow**.

This experiment is the **foundation** for all modern fluid dynamics classification and is the exact procedure replicated in the KTU Fluid Mechanics Lab.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Breakdown of Flow Regimes

### Step 1: Identification of Flow Type via Force Balance
Fluid flow is governed by the interplay of two principal forces:
1. **Inertial Forces** ($F_i \propto \rho V^2 A$) — These forces resist changes in velocity (Newton's Second Law applied to fluid elements).
2. **Viscous Forces** ($F_v \propto \mu V L$) — These forces resist deformation and tend to damp out motion.

### Step 2: Formation of the Dimensionless Ratio
By taking the ratio:

$$\frac{F_i}{F_v} \propto \frac{\rho V^2 A}{\mu V L} = \frac{\rho V L}{\mu} = \text{Re}$$

Since this ratio is **dimensionless**, it provides a universal basis for comparing flows of different fluids, pipes, and velocities.

### Step 3: Critical Reynolds Number ($Re_c$)
The **Critical Reynolds Number** is the threshold value of Re at which the flow regime changes. For a **circular straight pipe**:
- **Lower Critical $Re_c = 2000$** — Below this, flow is always laminar regardless of disturbances.
- **Upper Critical $Re_c = 4000$** — Above this, flow is always turbulent.
- **Between 2000 and 4000** — The flow is **transitional** and unstable.

### Step 4: Practical Significance in Engineering
- **Laminar Flow** → Predictable pressure drops, used in lubrication systems, capillary tubes, microfluidics.
- **Turbulent Flow** → Enhanced mixing, used in chemical reactors, heat exchangers, combustion chambers.
- **Transitional Flow** → Unreliable for design; engineers avoid this region.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Formula** | **Expression** | **Variables / Units** | **Application** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Reynolds Number | $\text{Re} = \dfrac{\rho V D}{\mu}$ | $\rho [\text{kg/m}^3]$, $V [\text{m/s}]$, $D [\text{m}]$, $\mu [\text{Pa·s}]$ | Direct calculation with viscosity known |
| Reynolds Number (Kinematic) | $\text{Re} = \dfrac{V D}{\nu}$ | $V [\text{m/s}]$, $D [\text{m}]$, $\nu [\text{m}^2/\text{s}]$ | Used when kinematic viscosity is given |
| Mean Velocity | $V = \dfrac{Q}{A} = \dfrac{4Q}{\pi D^2}$ | $Q [\text{m}^3/\text{s}]$, $A [\text{m}^2]$ | Convert volumetric flow to velocity |
| Discharge | $Q = \dfrac{V_t}{t}$ | $V_t [\text{mL or L}]$, $t [\text{s}]$ | Measured from collecting tank |
| Kinematic Viscosity (Water) | $\nu = \dfrac{\mu}{\rho}$ | $\mu [\text{Pa·s}]$, $\rho [\text{kg/m}^3]$ | For water at lab temperature |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Tip:** Always convert all units to **SI base units** before substitution. A common student error is using cm/s for velocity and mm for diameter without conversion.

### 2.3 Engineering Utility — Why This Experiment Matters

| **Industry / Field** | **Application of Reynolds Number** |
|---|---|
| **Petroleum Pipelines** | Determines pumping power and frictional losses |
| **Aerospace** | Airflow over wings (laminar flow = lower drag) |
| **Biomedical Engineering** | Blood flow in arteries (Re $\approx$ 1000 for healthy flow) |
| **Chemical Engineering** | Reactor design, mixing efficiency |
| **Civil Engineering** | Water supply networks, sewer design |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Universal Law:** If two flows have the **same Reynolds Number** and **geometrically similar boundaries**, they are **dynamically similar** — this is the foundation of model testing in engineering (e.g., wind tunnels, ship hulls).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Reynolds Number from Navier-Stokes Equations

### Step 1: Start with the Navier-Stokes Equation
The general momentum equation for an incompressible, Newtonian fluid in vector form:

$$\rho \left( \frac{\partial \vec{V}}{\partial t} + \vec{V} \cdot \nabla \vec{V} \right) = -\nabla p + \mu \nabla^2 \vec{V} + \rho \vec{g}$$

### Step 2: Identify the Dominant Terms
For **steady, fully-developed flow** in a pipe:
- The transient term $\dfrac{\partial \vec{V}}{\partial t} = 0$
- The convective (inertial) term $\vec{V} \cdot \nabla \vec{V} \neq 0$
- The viscous term $\mu \nabla^2 \vec{V} \neq 0$

### Step 3: Non-Dimensionalize the Equation
Introduce dimensionless variables using characteristic scales:
- $V^* = \dfrac{V}{V_0}$ (velocity scale)
- $L^* = \dfrac{L}{D}$ (length scale, where $D$ is pipe diameter)
- $p^* = \dfrac{p}{\rho V_0^2}$ (pressure scale)

Substituting into the momentum equation and dividing through by $\rho V_0^2 / D$:

$$\left( \vec{V}^* \cdot \nabla^* \vec{V}^* \right) = -\nabla^* p^* + \frac{1}{\text{Re}} \nabla^{*2} \vec{V}^*$$

### Step 4: Identify the Dimensionless Group
The coefficient of the viscous term is:

$$\frac{\mu}{\rho V_0 D} = \frac{1}{\text{Re}}$$

Therefore, the **Reynolds Number** emerges naturally as:

$$\boxed{\text{Re} = \frac{\rho V D}{\mu}}$$

## 3.2 Derivation of Mean Velocity from Discharge Measurements

### Step 1: Volumetric Flow Rate Definition
Discharge $Q$ is the volume of fluid passing per unit time:

$$Q = \frac{V_t}{t}$$

Where $V_t$ is the volume collected in the measuring tank and $t$ is the collection time.

### Step 2: Continuity Equation for Pipe Flow
For an incompressible fluid in a circular pipe:

$$Q = A \times V$$

Where $A$ is the cross-sectional area of the pipe.

### Step 3: Express Area for Circular Pipe
For a circular pipe of internal diameter $D$:

$$A = \frac{\pi D^2}{4}$$

### Step 4: Solve for Mean Velocity
Combining the above equations:

$$\begin{aligned}
V &= \frac{Q}{A} \\
&= \frac{V_t / t}{\pi D^2 / 4} \\
&= \frac{4 V_t}{\pi D^2 \, t}
\end{aligned}$$

This is the equation used in lab calculations to find the velocity from a stopwatch and measuring cylinder.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Worked Numerical Example (KTU Lab Standard)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Statement:**
> In a Reynolds Number apparatus, water at $27°C$ ($\rho = 996 \,\text{kg/m}^3$, $\mu = 0.85 \times 10^{-3} \,\text{Pa·s}$) flows through a glass tube of internal diameter $D = 25 \,\text{mm}$. The volumetric discharge is measured as $Q = 0.0004 \,\text{m}^3/\text{s}$. Determine the type of flow.

### Solution — Step-by-Step:

**Step 1: Identify Given Data**
- $D = 25 \,\text{mm} = 0.025 \,\text{m}$
- $Q = 0.0004 \,\text{m}^3/\text{s}$
- $\rho = 996 \,\text{kg/m}^3$
- $\mu = 0.85 \times 10^{-3} \,\text{Pa·s} = 8.5 \times 10^{-4} \,\text{Pa·s}$

**Step 2: Calculate Mean Velocity**

$$V = \frac{Q}{A} = \frac{4Q}{\pi D^2}$$

$$V = \frac{4 \times 0.0004}{\pi \times (0.025)^2}$$

$$V = \frac{0.0016}{\pi \times 0.000625}$$

$$V = \frac{0.0016}{0.0019635}$$

$$V \approx 0.815 \,\text{m/s}$$

**Step 3: Calculate Reynolds Number**

$$\text{Re} = \frac{\rho V D}{\mu}$$

$$\text{Re} = \frac{996 \times 0.815 \times 0.025}{8.5 \times 10^{-4}}$$

$$\text{Re} = \frac{20.30}{8.5 \times 10^{-4}}$$

$$\text{Re} \approx 23,882$$

**Step 4: Classify the Flow**

Since $\text{Re} = 23{,}882 \gg 4000$, the flow is **TURBULENT**.

### 3.4 Classification Decision Matrix

| **Computed Re Value** | **Flow Regime** | **Dye Behavior in Experiment** |
|---|---|---|
| $\text{Re} < 2000$ | **Laminar** | Dye travels as a straight, undisturbed line |
| $2000 \leq \text{Re} \leq 4000$ | **Transitional** | Dye wavers, oscillates, intermittent breakup |
| $\text{Re} > 4000$ | **Turbulent** | Dye diffuses rapidly, fills cross-section |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Reynolds Apparatus

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Overhead Constant Head Tank] --> B[Smooth Inlet Pipe with Stilling Section]
    B --> C[Transparent Test Pipe - Glass Tube]
    C --> D[Dye Injection Nozzle with Reservoir]
    C --> E[Flow Control Valve]
    E --> F[Measuring Tank with Calibration]
    F --> G[Weighing Balance or Volumetric Cylinder]
    F --> H[Stopwatch]
    H --> I[Data Acquisition Tabulation]
    D --> J[Dye Visualization Observation Window]
    J --> K{Laminar, Transitional, or Turbulent Judgment}
    I --> L[Calculation of Re for Each Trial]
    L --> M[Classification Tabulation and Graph Plot]
```

## 4.2 Schematic Process Flow of the Experiment

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Step 1: Set up apparatus, ensure steady overflow] --> P2[Step 2: Open valve slightly for low flow]
    P2 --> P3[Step 3: Inject dye via nozzle]
    P3 --> P4[Step 4: Observe dye pattern - LAMINAR]
    P4 --> P5[Step 5: Increase flow gradually]
    P5 --> P6[Step 6: Note onset of dye fluctuation - TRANSITIONAL]
    P6 --> P7[Step 7: Increase flow further]
    P7 --> P8[Step 8: Observe dye diffusion - TURBULENT]
    P8 --> P9[Step 9: For each state, measure V and t]
    P9 --> P10[Step 10: Calculate Re and tabulate results]
    P10 --> P11[Step 11: Plot Re vs V graph]
    P11 --> P12[Step 12: Identify critical Re from graph]
```

## 4.3 Functional Block Diagram — Calculation Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    IN1[Measured Volume Vt in mL] --> CALC1[Convert to m^3]
    IN2[Time t in seconds] --> CALC2[Discharge Q = Vt per t]
    CALC1 --> CALC2
    IN3[Diameter D in mm] --> CALC3[Convert to meters]
    CALC3 --> CALC4[Area A = pi D squared divided by 4]
    CALC2 --> CALC5[Velocity V = Q divided by A]
    CALC4 --> CALC5
    IN4[Density rho in kg per m^3] --> CALC6[Re = rho V D divided by mu]
    IN5[Viscosity mu in Pa.s] --> CALC6
    CALC5 --> CALC6
    CALC6 --> JUDGE{Re Comparison}
    JUDGE --> L1[Re less than 2000 - LAMINAR]
    JUDGE --> L2[Re between 2000 and 4000 - TRANSITIONAL]
    JUDGE --> L3[Re greater than 4000 - TURBULENT]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation Guide:**
> The block diagrams illustrate the sequential experimental procedure and the calculation chain. The student must trace each input (measured quantity) through the functional blocks to arrive at the final flow classification. This mirrors the exact procedure expected in the KTU lab record and viva voce.

## 4.4 Typical Reynolds Number Apparatus — Component Identification Table

| **Component** | **Function** | **Material** |
|---|---|---|
| Overhead Tank | Maintains constant head pressure | Mild Steel / PVC |
| Test Pipe | Transparent observation of flow | Borosilicate Glass |
| Dye Reservoir | Stores colored dye (KMnO₄ or ink) | Glass |
| Dye Injector | Releases dye at pipe center | Stainless Steel |
| Control Valve | Regulates flow rate | Brass / PVC |
| Measuring Tank | Collects water for volume measurement | Stainless Steel |
| Stopcock | Releases collected water | Brass |
| Thermometer | Monitors water temperature | Mercury / Digital |
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**Q: Define Reynolds Number and state its physical significance.**

**Model Answer:**
Reynolds Number is a dimensionless number that represents the ratio of **inertial forces to viscous forces** in a fluid flow. It is mathematically defined as:

$$\text{Re} = \frac{\rho V D}{\mu} = \frac{V D}{\nu}$$

**Physical Significance:**
1. It predicts whether the flow will be **laminar, transitional, or turbulent**.
2. It establishes the basis for **dynamic similarity** in model studies.
3. It enables engineers to scale laboratory results to real-world systems.

**[3 Marks: 1 for definition formula, 2 for physical significance points]**

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**Q: Differentiate between laminar and turbulent flow.**

**Model Answer:**

| **Parameter** | **Laminar Flow** | **Turbulent Flow** |
|---|---|---|
| Reynolds Number | $\text{Re} < 2000$ | $\text{Re} > 4000$ |
| Velocity Profile | Parabolic (for Hagen-Poiseuille) | Flat / Logarithmic |
| Fluid Layers | Move in parallel, no mixing | Eddies and cross-mixing present |
| Friction Factor | $f = 64 / \text{Re}$ | $f = 0.316 / \text{Re}^{0.25}$ (Blasius) |
| Pressure Drop | Directly proportional to V | Proportional to $V^{1.75}$ to $V^2$ |
| Dye Behavior | Straight line | Diffuses rapidly |

**[3 Marks: 1 for Re range, 2 for clear differentiation points]**

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**Q: In a Reynolds experiment conducted in the Fluid Mechanics Lab, water at $25°C$ flows through a horizontal glass tube of internal diameter $20 \,\text{mm}$. The discharge measured is $2.5 \,\text{L/min}$. The density and dynamic viscosity of water at $25°C$ are $997 \,\text{kg/m}^3$ and $0.89 \times 10^{-3} \,\text{Pa·s}$ respectively.**

**(a)** Calculate the Reynolds Number and identify the type of flow. **(7 Marks)**
**(b)** Describe the experimental procedure with a neat sketch, and state the observations for laminar, transitional, and turbulent flow. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Part (a):

**Given Data:**
- $D = 20 \,\text{mm} = 0.020 \,\text{m}$
- $Q = 2.5 \,\text{L/min} = \dfrac{2.5 \times 10^{-3}}{60} \,\text{m}^3/\text{s} = 4.167 \times 10^{-5} \,\text{m}^3/\text{s}$
- $\rho = 997 \,\text{kg/m}^3$
- $\mu = 0.89 \times 10^{-3} \,\text{Pa·s}$

**Step 1: Calculate Mean Velocity**

$$V = \frac{Q}{A} = \frac{4Q}{\pi D^2}$$

$$V = \frac{4 \times 4.167 \times 10^{-5}}{\pi \times (0.020)^2}$$

$$V = \frac{1.667 \times 10^{-4}}{\pi \times 4 \times 10^{-4}}$$

$$V = \frac{1.667 \times 10^{-4}}{1.2566 \times 10^{-3}}$$

$$V = 0.1326 \,\text{m/s}$$

**Step 2: Calculate Reynolds Number**

$$\text{Re} = \frac{\rho V D}{\mu}$$

$$\text{Re} = \frac{997 \times 0.1326 \times 0.020}{0.89 \times 10^{-3}}$$

$$\text{Re} = \frac{2.644}{0.89 \times 10^{-3}}$$

$$\text{Re} \approx 2971$$

**Step 3: Classification**

Since $2000 < 2971 < 4000$, the flow is **TRANSITIONAL FLOW**.

**[7 Marks: 2 for velocity calculation, 3 for Re calculation, 2 for classification with justification]**

---

#### Model Solution to Part (b):

**Experimental Procedure:**

1. **Apparatus Setup:** The apparatus consists of an overhead constant head tank connected to a horizontal transparent glass test pipe. A dye reservoir with a fine nozzle is fitted at the inlet section of the test pipe. A control valve regulates the flow rate, and the discharge is collected in a measuring tank.

2. **Preliminary Steps:** Fill the overhead tank and allow water to overflow until a steady constant head is achieved. This ensures a constant driving pressure throughout the experiment.

3. **Establishing Laminar Flow:** Open the control valve slightly to permit a small flow rate. Inject dye through the nozzle into the test pipe. Observe that the dye filament travels as a **straight, undisturbed line** parallel to the pipe axis — this confirms **laminar flow**.

4. **Transition Observation:** Gradually increase the flow rate by opening the valve further. At a critical flow rate, the dye filament begins to **waver and oscillate** — this indicates the **transitional regime**.

5. **Turbulent Flow Observation:** Open the valve fully. The dye filament **breaks up and diffuses** rapidly, mixing with the entire cross-section of water — confirming **turbulent flow**.

6. **Quantitative Measurements:** For each flow regime, collect water in the measuring tank for a fixed time interval (measured via stopwatch). Record volume $V_t$ and time $t$. Compute $Q$, then $V$, then $\text{Re}$, and tabulate results.

7. **Plotting:** Plot a graph of $\text{Re}$ vs $V$ to identify the critical values where transitions occur.

**[7 Marks: 3 for procedure steps, 2 for sketch description, 2 for observations in each regime]**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam - May 2023]

**Q: A Reynolds Number apparatus uses a glass tube of $15 \,\text{mm}$ bore. Water at $30°C$ ($\rho = 995.7 \,\text{kg/m}^3$, $\mu = 0.8 \times 10^{-3} \,\text{Pa·s}$) is allowed to flow through it. For a particular trial, the time taken to collect $500 \,\text{mL}$ of water is $40 \,\text{seconds}$.**

**(a)** Calculate the Reynolds Number for this trial and state the flow type. **(7 Marks)**
**(b)** Explain the significance of Reynolds Number in dynamic similarity and model testing with two engineering examples. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Part (a):

**Given Data:**
- $D = 15 \,\text{mm} = 0.015 \,\text{m}$
- $V_t = 500 \,\text{mL} = 500 \times 10^{-6} \,\text{m}^3 = 5 \times 10^{-4} \,\text{m}^3$
- $t = 40 \,\text{s}$
- $\rho = 995.7 \,\text{kg/m}^3$
- $\mu = 0.8 \times 10^{-3} \,\text{Pa·s}$

**Step 1: Calculate Discharge**

$$Q = \frac{V_t}{t} = \frac{5 \times 10^{-4}}{40} = 1.25 \times 10^{-5} \,\text{m}^3/\text{s}$$

**Step 2: Calculate Mean Velocity**

$$V = \frac{Q}{A} = \frac{4Q}{\pi D^2}$$

$$V = \frac{4 \times 1.25 \times 10^{-5}}{\pi \times (0.015)^2}$$

$$V = \frac{5 \times 10^{-5}}{\pi \times 2.25 \times 10^{-4}}$$

$$V = \frac{5 \times 10^{-5}}{7.0686 \times 10^{-4}}$$

$$V = 0.0707 \,\text{m/s}$$

**Step 3: Calculate Reynolds Number**

$$\text{Re} = \frac{\rho V D}{\mu}$$

$$\text{Re} = \frac{995.7 \times 0.0707 \times 0.015}{0.8 \times 10^{-3}}$$

$$\text{Re} = \frac{1.0562}{0.8 \times 10^{-3}}$$

$$\text{Re} \approx 1320$$

**Step 4: Classification**

Since $\text{Re} = 1320 < 2000$, the flow is **LAMINAR FLOW**.

**[7 Marks: 1 for discharge, 2 for velocity, 3 for Re, 1 for classification]**

---

#### Model Solution to Part (b):

**Significance of Reynolds Number in Dynamic Similarity:**

Dynamic similarity is the condition where the **force ratios** between a model and prototype are identical, ensuring that flow behavior in the model accurately predicts flow behavior in the full-scale prototype. The Reynolds Number is the **governing dimensionless group** for flows dominated by inertial and viscous forces.

**Condition for Dynamic Similarity:**

$$\text{Re}_{\text{model}} = \text{Re}_{\text{prototype}}$$

$$\frac{V_m D_m}{\nu_m} = \frac{V_p D_p}{\nu_p}$$

**Engineering Example 1: Submarine Hull Testing in a Towing Tank**
A scale model of a submarine (1:25 scale) is tested in a water towing tank. To achieve dynamic similarity, the model velocity $V_m$ must be 25 times higher than the prototype velocity $V_p$ (since $D_m = D_p/25$ and using the same fluid). This allows prediction of drag forces, boundary layer behavior, and pressure distribution on the full-scale submarine without building the actual vessel.

**Engineering Example 2: Aircraft Wing Testing in Wind Tunnels**
A 1:10 scale model of an aircraft wing is tested in a wind tunnel. To match the Reynolds Number of the actual aircraft in flight (which occurs at high altitude with different air viscosity), the wind tunnel air pressure is increased (compressing the air changes $\nu$), or the velocity is scaled appropriately. This allows engineers to measure lift, drag, and stall characteristics accurately.

**[7 Marks: 2 for concept explanation, 2 for condition, 1.5 per engineering example]**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Unit Inconsistency (–2 marks):** Mixing mm with m³, or mL with m³ without conversion. Always convert to SI units BEFORE substituting.
> 2. **Wrong Formula (–2 marks):** Using $\text{Re} = \dfrac{\mu}{\rho V D}$ instead of $\text{Re} = \dfrac{\rho V D}{\mu}$. **Memorize:** Re increases with $\rho$, $V$, and $D$.
> 3. **No Range Justification (–1 mark):** Simply stating "turbulent" without comparing the Re value to 2000 and 4000.
> 4. **Missing Sketch (–2 marks in Part B):** Sketches are compulsory in 14-mark questions. A labeled diagram of the apparatus earns full procedure marks.
> 5. **Ignoring Temperature Effect (–1 mark):** Viscosity changes with temperature; always record water temperature during the experiment and note it in the observation table.
> 6. **Using Kinematic Viscosity Incorrectly:** If $\nu$ is given in Stokes (cm²/s), convert to m²/s by dividing by $10^4$ before calculation.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU Board Exam Final Glance:**

- **Reynolds Number Definition:** $\text{Re} = \dfrac{\rho V D}{\mu} = \dfrac{V D}{\nu}$ — A **dimensionless number**.
- **For Circular Pipes:**
   - $\text{Re} < 2000$ → **Laminar**
   - $2000 < \text{Re} < 4000$ → **Transitional**
   - $\text{Re} > 4000$ → **Turbulent**
- **Mean Velocity Formula:** $V = \dfrac{4Q}{\pi D^2}$ where $Q = \dfrac{V_t}{t}$.
- **Diameter Always Internal (Bore):** Use inside diameter $D$ of the pipe, not outside diameter.
- **Dye Behavior:** Straight line = laminar, oscillating = transitional, diffusion = turbulent.
- **Experiment Inventor:** Osborne Reynolds, **1883**, University of Manchester.
- **Dye Used:** Potassium Permanganate ($\text{KMnO}_4$) solution or colored ink.
- **Apparatus Components:** Overhead tank, glass test pipe, dye injection system, control valve, measuring tank, stopwatch.
- **Dynamic Similarity:** When $\text{Re}_{\text{model}} = \text{Re}_{\text{prototype}}$, the flows are dynamically similar.
- **SI Unit Vigilance:** Convert mm → m, mL → m³, minutes → seconds BEFORE any calculation.
- **Temperature Dependency:** Both $\rho$ and $\mu$ vary with temperature; record water temperature at the start of each trial.
- **Graph to Plot:** Re vs $V$ — the critical velocity (and hence Re) is identified at the point of transition.
- **For Hagen-Poiseuille (Laminar):** Velocity profile is parabolic, $V_{max} = 2 V_{avg}$.
- **For Turbulent:** Velocity profile is much flatter (1/7th power law approximation).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Determination of Hydraulic Coefficients of Orifices Under Constant Head Method
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCNSL307 - FLUID MECHANICS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Determination of Hydraulic Coefficients of Orifices Under Constant Head Method

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

An **orifice** is a small opening made in the side or bottom of a vessel/tank through which a fluid discharges, with the diameter of the opening being **small compared to the head of the fluid** causing the flow. The edge of the orifice is generally sharp-edged and made in a flat plate.

A **mouthpiece** is a short pipe (length usually 2 to 3 times the diameter of the orifice) fitted to the orifice to modify the flow conditions.

The **hydraulic coefficients** are dimensionless constants that characterize the actual performance of an orifice relative to its ideal (theoretical) behavior. Three principal coefficients are determined experimentally:

| Coefficient | Symbol | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| Coefficient of Discharge | $C_d$ | Ratio of actual discharge to theoretical discharge |
| Coefficient of Velocity | $C_v$ | Ratio of actual velocity at vena contracta to theoretical velocity |
| Coefficient of Contraction | $C_c$ | Ratio of area of vena contracta to area of orifice |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Fundamental Relationship (Must Memorize):**
> $$C_d = C_c \times C_v$$
> This is the **master identity** linking all three hydraulic coefficients. Examiners almost always test this.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Garden Hose and the Pinhole"

Imagine you pierce a small hole at the bottom of a large water tank with a pin (orifice). According to Torricelli's theorem, water should shoot out at a velocity $\sqrt{2gH}$. In an **ideal frictionless world**, the jet would simply be a column of the same diameter as the hole, and the discharge would be exactly predicted.

But in **reality**:
1. Water, just before exiting, cannot make a sharp 90° turn — the streamlines curve inward, and the jet **shrinks** to a smaller cross-section called the **vena contracta**. This is why a garden hose stream is narrower than the tap opening.
2. **Friction** along the orifice edge and turbulence losses reduce the velocity below the theoretical value.
3. Some water also **swirls and eddies** near the entry, dissipating energy.

These three real-world losses are exactly what $C_c$, $C_v$, and $C_d$ quantify.

## 1.3 The "Constant Head" Philosophy

In the **constant head method**, the head $H$ maintained above the centerline of the orifice is **kept fixed** during the entire collection period using an overflow arrangement (a small weir or a supply tank with adjustable inflow). This eliminates the error introduced by a falling head, simplifying the calculation of theoretical discharge and ensuring the coefficients represent a **single, steady operating condition**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Values for a Sharp-Edged Circular Orifice (Textbook Reference):**
> - $C_c \approx 0.62$ to $0.65$
> - $C_v \approx 0.97$ to $0.98$
> - $C_d \approx 0.61$ to $0.64$
>
> Any experimental value lying in these bands indicates a successful experiment.

## 1.4 Vena Contracta — The "Neck" of the Jet

Immediately downstream of the orifice, the jet contracts to a minimum cross-sectional area called the **vena contracta**, located at a distance of approximately **0.5 × diameter of the orifice** from the orifice plane. At this section, the flow is nearly parallel and the pressure is approximately **atmospheric**.

```
   Tank wall |||||  →  →  →  jet shrinks  →  =  vena contracta  →  jet expands slightly
              |||||                       (minimum area = ac)
              |||||
   ───────────|||||──────────  Orifice plate (sharp edge)
              |||||      ↑
                       ~0.5 D
```

The cross-sectional area of the jet at vena contracta ($a_c$) is **less than** the orifice area ($a$), giving:
$$C_c = \frac{a_c}{a}$$

---

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation — Torricelli's Law

Applying Bernoulli's equation between a point (1) on the free surface of the tank and a point (2) at the vena contracta (taking the vena contracta as datum):

$$\frac{p_1}{\rho g} + \frac{v_1^2}{2g} + z_1 = \frac{p_2}{\rho g} + \frac{v_2^2}{2g} + z_2$$

**Assumptions of ideal flow:**
- $p_1 = p_2 = p_{atm}$ (both points at atmospheric pressure)
- $v_1 \approx 0$ (large tank, surface velocity negligible)
- $z_1 - z_2 = H$ (head above vena contracta)
- No losses (frictionless)

This reduces to:

$$H = \frac{v_{th}^2}{2g} \quad \Rightarrow \quad v_{th} = \sqrt{2gH}$$

## 2.2 The Three Hydraulic Coefficients — Detailed Logic

### (i) Coefficient of Velocity, $C_v$

Because of frictional and turbulence losses at the orifice edge, the **actual velocity** $v_a$ at vena contracta is **less than** the theoretical velocity $v_{th}$:

$$\boxed{C_v = \frac{v_a}{v_{th}} = \frac{v_a}{\sqrt{2gH}}}$$

For sharp-edged orifices, $C_v$ is typically **0.97–0.98**, indicating that velocity losses are small.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **How is $v_a$ measured?** It is **not** measured directly with a meter. Instead, it is computed from the **trajectory of the jet**. The jet issuing horizontally falls under gravity and traces a **parabolic path**:
> $$x^2 = 4 H_y' \cdot y$$
> where $H_y'$ is the vertical distance from the orifice centerline to the point of measurement, $x$ is the horizontal distance, and $y$ is the vertical drop. Substituting the geometry:
> $$v_a = x \sqrt{\frac{g}{2 y}}$$

### (ii) Coefficient of Contraction, $C_c$

The jet does not retain the full area of the orifice. At the vena contracta:

$$\boxed{C_c = \frac{a_c}{a}}$$

For sharp-edged circular orifices, $C_c \approx 0.62$ — this is the **biggest source of loss** for an orifice.

The area of the jet at vena contracta is computed as:
$$a_c = \frac{Q_{act}}{v_a}$$

where $Q_{act}$ is the actual discharge collected.

### (iii) Coefficient of Discharge, $C_d$

The **actual discharge** is the volume of water collected per unit time:

$$Q_{act} = \frac{V}{t} \quad \text{(where } V = \text{volume collected, } t = \text{time taken)}$$

The **theoretical discharge** assumes the entire orifice area passes fluid at theoretical velocity:

$$Q_{th} = a \cdot v_{th} = a \cdot \sqrt{2gH}$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{C_d = \frac{Q_{act}}{Q_{th}} = \frac{Q_{act}}{a \sqrt{2gH}}}$$

### (iv) The Identity

Since $Q_{act} = a_c \cdot v_a$ and $Q_{th} = a \cdot v_{th}$:

$$C_d = \frac{a_c \cdot v_a}{a \cdot v_{th}} = \underbrace{\frac{a_c}{a}}_{C_c} \cdot \underbrace{\frac{v_a}{v_{th}}}_{C_v} = C_c \cdot C_v$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** Orifice meters and nozzles used in industry to measure pipe flow rates rely on $C_d$ as a calibration constant. The value of $C_d$ depends on the geometry (sharp-edged, rounded, conical) and Reynolds number.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — High-Yield Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Theoretical velocity | $v_{th} = \sqrt{2gH}$ | m/s | From Torricelli |
| 2 | Actual velocity (trajectory) | $v_a = x\sqrt{g/(2y)}$ | m/s | From jet path |
| 3 | Coefficient of velocity | $C_v = v_a / \sqrt{2gH}$ | dimensionless | |
| 4 | Actual discharge | $Q_{act} = V/t$ | m³/s | Volume / Time |
| 5 | Theoretical discharge | $Q_{th} = a\sqrt{2gH}$ | m³/s | Ideal case |
| 6 | Coefficient of discharge | $C_d = Q_{act} / Q_{th}$ | dimensionless | |
| 7 | Vena contracta area | $a_c = Q_{act}/v_a$ | m² | |
| 8 | Coefficient of contraction | $C_c = a_c/a$ | dimensionless | |
| 9 | Identity | $C_d = C_c \cdot C_v$ | dimensionless | **Always true** |
| 10 | Jet trajectory | $x^2 = 4 H_y' \cdot y$ | m² | Parabola |
| 11 | Orifice area (circular) | $a = \pi d^2 / 4$ | m² | For circular orifice |
| 12 | Orifice area (rectangular) | $a = b \times d$ | m² | Width × depth |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Constants to remember (Bold required in answers):**
> - Acceleration due to gravity: **$g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$**
> - Water density: **$\rho = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$**
> - Kinematic viscosity of water at 20°C: **$\nu = 1.004 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}$**

## 2.4 Why "Constant Head"?

In a **falling head** experiment, $H$ decreases as the tank empties, so the theoretical discharge changes continuously. The constant-head method:
- Uses an **overflow weir** or a **regulated inflow** to keep $H$ fixed.
- Makes the average theoretical velocity **constant** during collection.
- Allows simple averaging: average $H$ over the run ≈ constant $H$.
- Yields **cleaner, more reproducible** values of $C_d$.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of $C_v$ from Jet Trajectory

**Setup:** The orifice discharges horizontally from the side of a tank. A coordinate system is set with origin at the vena contracta, $x$-axis horizontal (along jet direction), and $y$-axis vertically downward.

**Equations of motion (projectile):**

$$\begin{aligned}
x &= v_a \cdot t \\
y &= \frac{1}{2} g t^2
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 1:** Eliminate $t$ from the two equations. From the first: $t = x/v_a$. Substitute into the second:

$$y = \frac{1}{2} g \left(\frac{x}{v_a}\right)^2 = \frac{g x^2}{2 v_a^2}$$

**Step 2:** Solve for $v_a$:

$$v_a^2 = \frac{g x^2}{2 y} \quad \Rightarrow \quad v_a = x \sqrt{\frac{g}{2 y}}$$

**Step 3:** Substitute into the definition of $C_v$:

$$C_v = \frac{v_a}{v_{th}} = \frac{x \sqrt{g/(2y)}}{\sqrt{2gH}}$$

**Step 4:** Simplify:

$$\begin{aligned}
C_v &= \frac{x \sqrt{g/(2y)}}{\sqrt{2gH}} \\
    &= \frac{x}{\sqrt{4 y H}} \\
    &= \frac{x}{2\sqrt{yH}}
\end{aligned}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Working Formula for $C_v$:**
> $$\boxed{C_v = \frac{x}{2\sqrt{yH}}}$$
> where $x$ = horizontal distance to a point, $y$ = vertical fall to that same point, $H$ = head over the orifice centerline.

## 3.2 Numerical Worked Example (KTU Typical Style)

**Given Data:**
- Diameter of orifice $d = 10 \text{ mm} = 0.010 \text{ m}$
- Head over orifice $H = 300 \text{ mm} = 0.300 \text{ m}$
- Volume collected $V = 4.5 \text{ litres} = 4.5 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3$
- Time of collection $t = 25 \text{ s}$
- Horizontal distance to measuring point $x = 250 \text{ mm} = 0.250 \text{ m}$
- Vertical fall $y = 75 \text{ mm} = 0.075 \text{ m}$
- $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$

**Step 1 — Area of orifice:**

$$a = \frac{\pi d^2}{4} = \frac{\pi (0.010)^2}{4} = 7.854 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 2 — Theoretical velocity:**

$$v_{th} = \sqrt{2gH} = \sqrt{2 \times 9.81 \times 0.300} = \sqrt{5.886} = 2.426 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 3 — Theoretical discharge:**

$$Q_{th} = a \cdot v_{th} = (7.854 \times 10^{-5}) \times 2.426 = 1.905 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

**Step 4 — Actual discharge:**

$$Q_{act} = \frac{V}{t} = \frac{4.5 \times 10^{-3}}{25} = 1.800 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

**Step 5 — Coefficient of discharge:**

$$C_d = \frac{Q_{act}}{Q_{th}} = \frac{1.800 \times 10^{-4}}{1.905 \times 10^{-4}} = 0.9449$$

**Step 6 — Actual velocity from trajectory:**

$$v_a = x \sqrt{\frac{g}{2y}} = 0.250 \times \sqrt{\frac{9.81}{2 \times 0.075}} = 0.250 \times \sqrt{65.4} = 0.250 \times 8.087 = 2.022 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 7 — Coefficient of velocity:**

$$C_v = \frac{v_a}{v_{th}} = \frac{2.022}{2.426} = 0.833$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Numerical Alert:** The computed $C_v = 0.833$ is unusually low. In a real sharp-edged orifice, $C_v$ should be **0.97–0.98**. This indicates a likely **measurement error** — perhaps the jet coordinates were measured inaccurately. This kind of result loses marks unless the student comments on it.

**Step 8 — Coefficient of contraction (using identity):**

$$C_c = \frac{C_d}{C_v} = \frac{0.9449}{0.833} = 1.134$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Conceptual Alert:** $C_c$ **cannot exceed 1.0** for an orifice (contraction always reduces area). This further confirms measurement error. Students must verify $C_c \leq 1$ before finalizing the result.

## 3.3 Experimental Procedure — Step-by-Step

1. **Set up the apparatus:** Fill the supply tank. Open the inflow valve and adjust the overflow arrangement to maintain a constant head over the orifice.
2. **Measure the head $H$:** Use a piezometer or hook gauge to measure the water level above the orifice centerline.
3. **Measure the orifice diameter $d$** using a vernier caliper.
4. **Allow flow to stabilize** — let water run for a minute so steady conditions are achieved.
5. **Trajectory measurement:** Place a set-square against a vertical scale; record $(x, y)$ coordinates of the jet path at 4–5 points. Verify $x^2/y$ is approximately constant (= $2v_a^2/g$).
6. **Discharge measurement:** Simultaneously start a stopwatch and collect water in a measuring tank for a fixed duration $t$ (typically 30–60 s).
7. **Record the volume $V$** collected.
8. **Repeat** the procedure for 3–4 different constant heads $H$.
9. **Calculate** $C_d$, $C_v$, $C_c$ for each trial; tabulate and average.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Sources of Error

| Source | Effect on $C_d$ | Effect on $C_v$ | Mitigation |
|---|---|---|---|
| Jet spreading / air drag | — | Decreases | Measure near vena contracta |
| Fluctuating head | Variable $H$ | Variable | Use overflow weir |
| Parallax in reading $x,y$ | — | Erratic | Use plumb line + set-square |
| Splashing in measuring tank | Increases $V$ | — | Use calming baffle |
| Orifice not sharp-edged | $C_v$ increases | — | Inspect before experiment |
