## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question Paper Style]**

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**Differentiate between a gate valve and a globe valve with respect to (i) flow direction, (ii) head loss when fully open, and (iii) typical application.**

**Model Answer:**

| Property | Gate Valve | Globe Valve |
|----------|-----------|-------------|
| Flow direction | Straight-through | Z-shaped, changes direction twice |
| Head loss (fully open) | Very low ($K \approx 0.15$) | Very high ($K \approx 10.0$) |
| Throttling capability | Poor — not recommended | Excellent — designed for throttling |
| Typical application | Main isolation in water mains, oil pipelines | Flow regulation, cooling water circuits, boiler feed |

---

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**What is the function of a check valve? Name any two types of check valves.**

**Model Answer:**
A **check valve** (also called a non-return valve) is a self-actuating valve that allows fluid to flow in **only one direction** and automatically prevents reverse flow when downstream pressure exceeds upstream pressure. It does not require external actuation.

**Two Types:**
1. **Swing Check Valve** — uses a hinged disc that swings open with forward flow and closes by gravity/backflow.
2. **Lift Check Valve** — uses a guided disc that lifts off the seat when flow is forward and reseats on flow reversal.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

#### **Question A — Option 1 (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the **construction and working of a globe valve**. Why is it preferred for throttling applications? *(7 Marks)*

**(b)** A horizontal pipe of 75 mm diameter carries water at a velocity of 2.5 m/s. The pipeline has a fully open globe valve ($K = 10$), a 90° standard elbow ($K = 0.9$), and a sudden contraction from 75 mm to 50 mm ($K = 0.35$, based on higher velocity). Determine:
   (i) the total minor head loss.
   (ii) the equivalent length of the fittings ($f = 0.025$). *(7 Marks)*

**Model Answer:**

**(a) Construction and Working of a Globe Valve:**

A globe valve consists of a spherical (globe-shaped) body with an internal baffle that separates it into two halves. A **stem** passes through the top, carrying a **disc or plug** at its lower end. The plug mates with a precisely machined **seat** (a circular opening in the baffle).

**Working:**
- When the handwheel is rotated clockwise, the stem descends, pushing the plug onto the seat → **valve closes**.
- When rotated anticlockwise, the stem rises, lifting the plug off the seat → **valve opens**.

Fluid enters through the **inlet (Z-direction)**, is forced to change direction by **180°** (Z-shape), passes through the seat opening, and exits through the outlet. This tortuous path creates significant turbulence, hence high head loss.

**Why preferred for throttling:**
- The plug-to-seat geometry provides **precise linear control** of flow area.
- Fine stem-thread pitch gives **gradual throttling**.
- The plug can be held in *any* intermediate position without seat damage.

---

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**Given:**
- $D_1 = 75 \text{ mm} = 0.075 \text{ m}$, $D_2 = 50 \text{ mm} = 0.050 \text{ m}$
- $V = 2.5 \text{ m/s}$ (in 75 mm pipe), $f = 0.025$

**Step 1 — Velocity in the 50 mm section (continuity):**

$$
A_1 V_1 = A_2 V_2
$$

$$
\frac{\pi}{4} (0.075)^2 \times 2.5 = \frac{\pi}{4} (0.050)^2 \times V_2
$$

$$
V_2 = 2.5 \times \left(\frac{0.075}{0.050}\right)^2 = 2.5 \times 2.25 = 5.625 \text{ m/s}
$$

**Step 2 — Loss coefficients:**

- $K_{\text{globe}} = 10.0$ (based on upstream velocity $V_1$)
- $K_{\text{elbow}} = 0.9$ (based on $V_1$)
- $K_{\text{contraction}} = 0.35$ (based on **higher** velocity $V_2$ as specified)

**Step 3 — Compute individual head losses:**

Globe valve:

$$
h_{L1} = 10.0 \times \frac{(2.5)^2}{2 \times 9.81} = 10.0 \times 0.3186 = 3.186 \text{ m}
$$

Elbow:

$$
h_{L2} = 0.9 \times 0.3186 = 0.287 \text{ m}
$$

Sudden contraction (using $V_2$):

$$
h_{L3} = 0.35 \times \frac{(5.625)^2}{2 \times 9.81} = 0.35 \times 1.612 = 0.564 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 4 — Total minor head loss:**

$$
h_{L,\text{total}} = 3.186 + 0.287 + 0.564 = 4.037 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 5 — Equivalent length:**

For the contraction, we must convert $K$ to the same reference. Using the upstream velocity:

$$
K_{\text{contraction}}' = 0.35 \times \left(\frac{V_2}{V_1}\right)^2 = 0.35 \times (2.25)^2 \cdot \text{(correction not needed as K is already in $V_2$ terms)}
$$

Direct equivalent length using upstream velocity references:

$$
L_e = \frac{D_1}{f} \left(K_{\text{globe}} + K_{\text{elbow}} + K_{\text{contraction,ref to }V_1}\right)
$$

For contraction referenced to $V_1$, divide $K$ by area ratio squared — but simpler: use total $K$ in upstream velocity head terms:

$$
\sum K_{\text{upstream}} = 10.0 + 0.9 + 0.35 \times 2.25 = 12.6875
$$

$$
L_e = \frac{0.075}{0.025} \times 12.6875 = 3 \times 12.6875 = 38.06 \text{ m}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning (Common Pitfall):** Many students use the **same velocity** for the sudden contraction without considering continuity. Always remember that the loss coefficient $K$ is referenced to a specific velocity — typically the **higher velocity** downstream for sudden contraction. Failing to apply continuity at $V_2 = 5.625$ m/s leads to **incorrect head loss** and loss of 3 marks.

---

#### **Question B — Option 2 (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Explain with sketches: **(i) Swing check valve**, **(ii) Gate valve**. Compare them on the basis of flow direction change, head loss, and application. *(7 Marks)*

**(b)** A pipeline of 100 mm diameter carries oil ($SG = 0.85$) at a flow rate of 12 L/s. The pipe includes 4 standard elbows ($K = 0.9$ each), 2 gate valves ($K = 0.15$ each), and a sudden expansion to 150 mm diameter ($K = 0.6$, based on lower velocity). Calculate:
   (i) Velocity in both pipe sections.
   (ii) Total minor head loss in metres of oil.
   (iii) Pressure drop across the fittings in kPa. *(7 Marks)*

**Model Answer:**

**(a) Explanation:**

**(i) Swing Check Valve:**
A swing check valve has a **hinged disc** that swings on a pivot inside the valve body. When flow is forward, the disc swings open, allowing fluid passage. When flow reverses, the disc is pushed back onto the seat by gravity and back-pressure, sealing the passage.

- **Flow direction change:** None — straight-through.
- **Head loss (open):** Moderate ($K \approx 2.5$).
- **Application:** Pump discharge lines, prevention of backflow into overhead tanks.

**(ii) Gate Valve:**
A gate valve has a **wedge-shaped gate** that slides perpendicular to the flow direction on a threaded stem. Turning the handwheel lowers or raises the gate. When fully open, the gate retracts fully into the bonnet, leaving a clear, unobstructed bore.

- **Flow direction change:** None — straight-through.
- **Head loss (open):** Very low ($K \approx 0.15$).
- **Application:** Main isolation in long pipelines; not for throttling.

| Property | Swing Check | Gate Valve |
|----------|-------------|------------|
| Actuation | Automatic | Manual / Actuator |
| Flow direction | Straight | Straight |
| Head loss | Moderate | Very low |
| Throttling | Not suitable | Not suitable |
| Backflow prevention | Yes | No |
| Primary use | Non-return | Isolation |

---

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**Given:**
- $D_1 = 100 \text{ mm} = 0.1 \text{ m}$
- $D_2 = 150 \text{ mm} = 0.15 \text{ m}$
- $Q = 12 \text{ L/s} = 0.012 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$
- $SG = 0.85$, $\rho_{\text{oil}} = 850 \text{ kg/m}^3$

**Step 1 — Velocity in section 1:**

$$
V_1 = \frac{Q}{A_1} = \frac{0.012}{\frac{\pi}{4}(0.1)^2} = \frac{0.012}{0.007854} = 1.528 \text{ m/s}
$$

**Step 2 — Velocity in section 2 (continuity):**

$$
V_2 = V_1 \times \left(\frac{D_1}{D_2}\right)^2 = 1.528 \times \left(\frac{0.1}{0.15}\right)^2
$$

$$
V_2 = 1.528 \times 0.4444 = 0.679 \text{ m/s}
$$

**Step 3 — Identify all K-values:**

- 4 elbows: $K = 4 \times 0.9 = 3.6$ (referenced to $V_1$)
- 2 gate valves: $K = 2 \times 0.15 = 0.3$ (referenced to $V_1$)
- 1 sudden expansion: $K = 0.6$ (referenced to **lower** velocity $V_2$)

**Step 4 — Compute head losses in oil column:**

Velocity head in section 1:

$$
\frac{V_1^2}{2g} = \frac{(1.528)^2}{2 \times 9.81} = \frac{2.335}{19.62} = 0.1190 \text{ m of oil}
$$

Velocity head in section 2:

$$
\frac{V_2^2}{2g} = \frac{(0.679)^2}{19.62} = \frac{0.461}{19.62} = 0.0235 \text{ m of oil}
$$

Head loss for elbows + gate valves (in $V_1$ section):

$$
h_{L,\text{elbows+valves}} = (3.6 + 0.3) \times 0.1190 = 3.9 \times 0.1190 = 0.464 \text{ m}
$$

Head loss for sudden expansion (in $V_2$):

$$
h_{L,\text{expansion}} = 0.6 \times 0.0235 = 0.0141 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 5 — Total minor head loss:**

$$
h_{L,\text{total}} = 0.464 + 0.0141 = 0.478 \text{ m of oil}
$$

**Step 6 — Convert to pressure drop (kPa):**

$$
\Delta P = \rho g h_L = 850 \times 9.81 \times 0.478
$$

$$
\Delta P = 8338.5 \times 0.478 = 3986 \text{ Pa} \approx 3.99 \text{ kPa}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning (Common Pitfall):** Students frequently **forget the direction of velocity reference** in sudden expansion. The K-value for sudden expansion ($K = 0.6$ in this case) is referenced to the **lower velocity side** ($V_2$). Using $V_1$ for the expansion loss will yield an incorrect answer and lose 2 marks.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (KTU PCNSL307 – Module 2)**

- A **valve** controls flow; a **fitting** changes direction, branches, or joins pipe sections.
- **Gate valve** = best isolation; **Globe valve** = best throttling; **Ball valve** = quick on/off; **Check valve** = backflow prevention; **Butterfly valve** = compact isolation for large pipes.
- **Elbow** changes direction; **Tee** branches; **Reducer** changes diameter; **Flange** joins large pipes; **Union** allows easy disconnection.
- **Head loss formula:** $h_L = K \cdot \dfrac{V^2}{2g}$ — must know this *cold* for viva.
- **Equivalent length formula:** $L_e = \dfrac{K \cdot D}{f}$.
- **Major loss > Minor loss** in long straight pipes; **Minor loss > Major loss** in short pipes with many fittings.
- **Globe valve has the highest K (~10)** — remember this for ranking questions.
- **Gate and ball valves** have the lowest K (~0.05–0.15) — best for isolation.
- A **swing check valve** is *automatic*; it requires no external control.
- **Sudden contraction** loss coefficient is referenced to the **higher (downstream) velocity**.
- **Sudden expansion** loss coefficient is referenced to the **lower (downstream) velocity**.
- **Diaphragm valves** are used for corrosive and sanitary fluids.
- **Pressure relief valves** are mandatory in boilers and pressurized systems (ASME code).
- For viva: always relate the *K-value table* to *why pumping cost increases* with poor fitting selection.

> [!NOTE]
> **Practical Lab Tip:** When recording observations in your PCNSL307 lab record, always (1) sketch the valve/fitting neatly with *labelled parts*, (2) state at least two real-world applications, and (3) tabulate the typical K-value. Examiners award full marks for structured, *neat, labelled diagrams*.
